attention of logicians and metaphysicians.  My three categories
are nothing but Hegel's three grades of thinking.  I know very
well that there are other categories, those which Hegel calls
by that name.  But I never succeeded in satisfying myself with
any list of them.  We may classify objects according to their
matter; as wooden things, iron things, silver things, ivory things,
etc.  But classifications according to structure {add}is{/add} are more important.
And it is the same with ideas.  Much as I would like to see Hegel's
list of categories reformed, I hold that a classification of the
elements of thought and consciousness according to their formal
structure is more important.  I believe in inventing new philosophical
words in order to avoid the ambiguities of the familiar words.
I use the word phane{add}r{/add}on to mean all that is present to the mind in
any sense or in any way whatsoever, regardless of whether it be fact
or figment.  I examine the phane{add}r{/add}on and I endeavor to sort out its
elements according to the complexity of their structure.  I thus
reach my three categories... I find that there are in the phanenon,
1st, elements each of which is (that is, appears to be, for we are
speaking of phenera) such as it is positively and regardless of
anything else.  I call these priman elements;
2nd, elements each of which is such as it is relatively to some
thing over against it, regardless of any third.  Such an element
may also have a character in itself, a priman character, inseperable

[unclear: Delete remainder]

9

from what it is to the other. But however inseperable that
priman character may be, it can be distinguished from the Secundan
element, which is the designation I give to that which is such as
it is to another, regardless of any third.
3rd, elements each of which is {add}wh{/add} at it is to a second and for a
third, that is, its very being, as phaneron, consists in its
bringing that second and that third into relationship. Such an
element I call a Tertian element.
You naturally expect Quartan elements, and so forth. But I not
merely opine, but can strictly demonstrate that every quartan part
of a phaneron is reducible to a combination of Tertian elements.
You might thoughtlessly ask why then a Tertian part cannot be
reduced to a combination of Secundan elements. But a moment's re-
flection will show that this is a senseless and purely verbal suggestion,
inasmuch as combination is already a Tertian idea.
Thus it can be made mathematically evident that elements of
the phaneron of these three grades of structure there may be, and
that none others can there be.
So, without looking deeper (for a deeper look might modify our
notion) we may naturally expect to find seven different schools of
metaphysicians, one for every day of the week.
If you will permit a little nonsense to relieve this solemn
subject we can {add}assign{/add} a particular day to each school. The meter-
physician ought to begin by trying the simplest hypothesis first,
that is, by admitting only Priman elements, as the Beskeleians and
Hedonists do, and in their doing this fiat lux, light, and a
splendid light was brought upon all the chaotic notions of human

society which had prevailed before Becaria and Bentham.  So you
will permit me to call this school the Sunday School.  Thus blazed
this extreme nominalism ought, and I should think, must pass, per-
haps insensibly, into the lesser light of Philistine, ordinary,
materialistic, and stoical nominalism, which admits Secundan ele-
ments and a firmament of existence, which consists only in struggle.
This will be the School of Monday.  But after all, those Priman ele-
ments effect nothing.  They are mere epiphenomena.  This reflexion
will produce the arid school of pure {add}d{/add}ynamism, mere brutal Mars,
highly appropriate for Tuesday.  But Tertian elements, elements
derived from life, evolutionary influences will slip into the ex-
planations of dynamism.  Mars will give way to Mercurius, and we
have a Spencerian Wednesday school.  But then ought to come the re-
flexion, Why retain these Secundan elements, since it is the Tertian
that suffice to effect everything, and the result will be a Hegel-
ian intellectualism, the Begriff the sole constituent.  This will
be the di{add}e{/add}s Jovis.  But then it will be felt that, after all, the
Priman elements must be admitted, and we shall have that neo{add}-H{/add}egelian-
ism of Royce and others who admit Priman along with their Tertian
elements, but cannot see that there is any brute force in the uni-
verse at all.  They make the world a law court in which the Priman
elements are the witnesses and the Tertian element the judge.  But
as for the sheriff, il commissario, with his sword and his shackles,
they do not see that he is {add}not{/add} a mere emanation of the judge's mind.  A
soft Venus world is their idea.  They belong to the day of Frigga.
At last comes the return to Aristotelianism, which recognizes that

11

an account of the world must admit Priman, Secundan, and Tertian
elements.  In that there is rest.  It is the day of old Saturn.
Of course, this is mere arbitrary fancy; but it has given me
an opportunity to point out something of the aspects of these
three kinds of elements.  Now let us endeavor to specify examples
of the three elements in consciousness.  The Priman elements of
consciousness are the pure qualities of feeling, red, green, the
harmony of a chord, the quality of a tooth-ache, its insistency
being left out of account, the glow of a contemplation of any sit-
uation, as mere quality.  That wherein the perception of red and
the imagination or memory of red agree and are the same is Priman.
As an example of a Secundan element, take, effort, which can only
be in so far as there is a resistance to it.  Without a counter-
resistance there is no effort.  Also, the shock of a surprise is
of this nature.  In a long sea-voyage in the trade-winds, one day
just like another, I get up some morning and coming on deck see
something quite novel.  The inertia of habitual expectation is
borne down, but not without a brief struggle; for I rub my eyes,
I shake myself, before I admit the unexpected to be real.  Experience
may be defined as the cognitive element which the course of life
has brutally forced upon me, without reason.  It implies a conserva-
tive, inert clinging to former ideas which has been conquered.  This
purely brute force is Secundan.  If it had any reason, it would be
Tertian.  But even when a stone falls in accordance with the law of
gravity, the mere law, of itself, is of no avail.  It is like a judge
on the bench without any sheriff.  The sheriff obeys the judge, it is
true.  Nevertheless, it is the brute force of his arm that acts and

that brute force, considered apart from the reason behind it, is
Secundan.  That mode of being which we call existence, the reac-
tion of everything in the universe against every other, the crowd-
ing out of a place for itself, acting most on things near, less on
things far, but brutally insisting on a place is Secundan.  I say
"brutally", because no law, so far as we know, makes any single
object to exist, law only determines in what way things shall behave,
once they do exist.

     The Tertian elements are exemplified most clearly in the action
of signs.  An orderly or aide-de-camp from a general rides up to a
colonel.  He sends into his ear some slight aerial vibrations {del}whose{/del}
whose energy is so slight that no other instrument than an ear could
detect them at all.  How is this energy transformed?  It sets a
million men in fierce, heroic, ebriate, life-prodigal violence against
another million, overthrows a government, gives hundreds of millions
of people an altogether different life.  How is this done?  By brute
force?  No; by force of logic.  Sitting here writing in my study, I
find forced upon my attention that the air of my room is, as we say
in English, "stuffy", that is, just beginning to embarrass respira-
tions without any definite recognizable quality.  I reason.  I say to
myself, This should not be; it is unfavorable to work.  How remedy it?
The window must be opened.  If the window is to be opened, I must
open it.  To open the window, I must be near it.  To be near it, I
must cross the room.  To cross the room, I must rise from my chair.
To rise from my chair I must bend my trunk and contract the muscles
of my legs after planting my feet under my chair.  About there I lose

13

{del}lose{/del} the power of recognizing my reasoning and the next thing I
am aware of is that I am moving across the room.  My muscles seem
to be making complicated systematic contractions; but I am sure I
do not know what they are doing.  I have only in mind my purpose
to have the air let in.  I forget even that, my mind going back to
what I am writing; but I still move to the window and the next
thing I know I {add}am{/add} taking up my pen.  I have to look up to mak{add}e{/add} sure
that I did open the window.  But of course I did.  A sign cannot
function at all without producing a physical effect.  All our
thoughts of every description are signs.  A sign is triadic because
it determines an interpretant sign of the same object to which it
refers itself.  A sign is thus a sign of an object, for an interpretant.
     Now I find,--at any rate, forty years of industrious and sceptical
investigation have confirmed me in thinking{add};{/add}- that every Secundan has
two varieties, a more "genuine", more Secundan Secundan, and a more
"degenerate", more Priman Secundan.  And the genuine Secundan Secundan
has in turn two varieties of the same sort, and so on, I cannot say how
far this goes.  Moreover, what is much more unexpected, instead of
these being six or seven different grades of Tertian, as I should have
confidently anticipated (and did, in fact, long endeavor to detect,) I
find there are only three, the Priman Tertian, the Secundan Tertian,
and the genuine Tertian.  Of the Secundan Tertian there are two grades,
the degenerate and the genuine.  Of the genuine Tertian there are three
grades, and so forth.  The Priman admits of no variation of form.
     Before giving examples of these things, I must say that although
there is but one pure Priman, there are impure and pseudo Primans, as

well as Pseudo Secundans.  For example, a Leibnizian monad is
something which is represented to be all that it is in itself,
by itself, and for itself.  But this is self-contradictory.  If
the monad really were for itself alone one could have no more
conception of it than one has of the inner life of a {del}fable{/del} {add}Pebble{/add} on the
beach.  It would be simply nothing to us.  I say, then, that the
monad has an "imputed" Primanity, or that it is a psuedo-priman.
It is represented to be Priman; but, just as being represented,
it is really Tertian.  Just so,a book upon exact logic might begin
with this sentence: "Consider a number of individual objects, A, B,
C, D, etc, all exactly alike and indistinguishable".  Here again
the ideas are vague, or indefinite, and like all vague objects are
not subject to the principle of contradiction (in every respect,
although they must be so in some respects, for no thought can be
perfectly vague.)  The objects A, B. C, D, which are said to be
absolutely indistinguishable (see for example, Kempe's great memoir
in the Philosophical Transactions for 1886, p. 14, section 73 where
he studies minutely collections of objects "undistinguished from
one another in dress or other circumstance".) are in the very same
breath distinguished as A. B. C. etc.; and indeed it is these dis-
tinct signs alone that enable us to have any sort of notion of the
indistinguishable and essentially vague objects to which they are
supposed to be attached.  This is another case of imputed Primanity.
So likewise in all those relations called "concurrencies", as when
ordinary limitative adjectives are regarded as relative terms, the
Secundanity is imputed merely.  These dyadic relations called "ref-
erences", such as the references of attribute to subject, are another
kind of imputed Secundanity.  There is, moreover, another kind of

15

impure Primanity.  Consider, a real individual object, as it is
in itself. This is Priman, being such as it is in itself, re-
gardless of other things. Yet this Primanity is not the original,
essential, Primanity of a quality of feeling, but is merely due to
the otherness of the individual from everything else in the uni-
verse.

I will {add}w{/add}not give some examples of the difference between degen-
erate and genuine Secundanity, and of the differences between Primen,
Secundan, and genuine Tertianity. A genuine Secundanity affects the
very mode of being of the Second. An effort cannot occur at all
without a resistance. A father is not a father if his son dies, and
has not that mode of being that he had when his son lived. A man
who is taller than another is not really taller unless the shorter
man exists. But that Secundanity which consists in one man's having
a stature of six feet and another man's having a stature of five feet
is a degenerate Secondanity, since each would be just what he is if
the other were not there, and would be Second in the same way to a
merely possible but non-existent man. The genuine Secundanity divides
again into a more and{add}a{/add}less genuine kind. Being "taller" is a genuine
Secundanity. Yet, after all, it is merely what is essentially involved
in two distinct facts each relating to a single individual. It might
be said to be degenerately genuine as compared with the Secundanity of
two brothers; for this cannot be resolved into two facts involving
only the two individuals. Still, it is not so genuine as the Secundanity
of Cain's killing Abel; since after all their brother head resulted
from the two facts that Cain was the son of Adam and Eve and that Abel

16

was the son of Adam and Eve. The killing cannot be resolved into
two separate facts.  Still, even this Secondanity is not so gen-
uine as that of effort and resistance, since Cain would have been
Cain just the same even if he had not killed Abel.
     Without fatiguing you with all the divisions of Priman and
Secundan Tertianity, I will merely remark, what everybody can think
out for himself, that Priman Tertianity is that Tertianity which
consists in possibilities and Secundan Tertianity is that Tertianity
which consists in brute, physical, or quasi-physical facts, while
genuine Tertianity consists in rational truths and understandings.
The genuine Tertian is again Primanly genuine, Secundanly genuine,
and genuinely genuine Tertians, which last are signs.  A sign is
something which is Secundan to an Object and determines an Inter-
pretant to be correspondingly Secundan to the same Object.  But we
can distinguish two Objects; the object as it is represented to be,
and the object as it is.  We can also distinguish three Interpretants;
the Interpretant as it is in itself regardless of the sign, the In-
terpretant as it is actually [unclear: caused?] by the sign, and the Interpretant
as the sign represents it to be intended.
     Signs may be divided in three ways; first, according to their
modes of being as Entia in themselves; second, according to their
relations to their Objects; third, according to the mode of their
appeal to their interpretants.
     A sign in itself may be an indefinite possibility, when I term
as a Qualisign, or it may be an existent thing or event, when I term
it a Sinsign [sin is the sino of simul, simplex, etc], or it may be
a general type, when I call it a legi-sign.  For example, a metaphor

17

        of a given description may have been actually employed or it may not.
      In any case, it is as a posibility, as a possible description, we
      will suppose, of a physical motion, that it is a sign of a state of
      feeling.  I call that a qualisign.  Chiaroscuro is a qualisign.
      The word il may occur twenty times on an Italian page.  Each occur-
      rence of it is a separate sign, and all the occurrences are instances
      of one and the same sign.  The occurrences are so many sinsigns.
      The one word is a legisign.  It is not indefinite, like chiaroscuro
      in general.

            In their relations to their objects, signs are divisible in two
      ways, according as we have in view the object as it is or the object as
      represented.  The former division is merely into signs of possibilities,
      signs of existents, and signs of types.  The other division is more
      important.  It is into, {add}1st{/add} first, icons, or these signs which represent
      their objects by virtue of a resemblance or analogy with them, such as
                                      [a, b, c,   ]
      a mathematical diagram, say     [a', b', c' ]     to represent their objects
                                      [a'', b'', c'']
      by virtue of being connected with them in fact, like a clock, or a

Third = Symbols, or those signs which represent their objects simply
because they will be interpreted to refer to those objects, like all
conventional and natural signs.

In their appeals to their interpretants signs are divisible in
three ways, according as we have in view the interpretant, as it is
in itself, or as it is the actual effect of the sign, or as it is
intended (or professed to be intended) by the sign.

Carnegie Institution.
Washington, D.C.

May 13, 1903.

Hon. Herbert H.D. Peirce,
State Department,   Washington, D.C.

Dear Sir:

At the meeting of the Executive Committee, held May 12, 1903,
the following resolution was passed:—

RESOLVED, That the Executive Committee, having under considera-
tion the question of payment for the preparation of a treatise on
logic, decided that, in view of the very large demands upon the funds
of the Institution for work in the fields of the natural sciences,
which it appears desirable to promote as far as possible, it does not
deem it expedient to employ any of the funds of the Carnegie Institution
in payment for the preparation of a work on this subject.

There were several applications for aid on file, and this resolution
applies to all of them, including that of your brother, Mr. Charles S.
Peirce.  The various manuscripts sent by him to the Institution for ex-
amination are now in the safe.  Will you kindly advise us what disposi-
tion to make of them?

Very truly yours

CHAS. D. WALCOTT,
Secretary.

I shall be asked why I have published so little and in
fragmentary a way. I answer,
        1st, that I have had extreme difficulty in getting what I
wrote on logic printed. My boxes are full of unprinted MSS on the
subject as carefully written as anything I ever wrote. Only those
things could be printed which could pass as relating to some other
subject, and then only if it was made so brief as to almost unin-
telligible, or else worked up so as to answer the purposes of
popular magazines.
        2nd, that even so, I have not been able to learn that as many
as half a dozen persons have ever read any paper of mine, no matter
how I had dressed it up.
        3rd, that during all these years the vast volume of my
results has been such that it has not been easy for me, with my
aptitude for the subject, my personal interest in the discoveries,
and my incessant study of them, to hold them all in my head at once
in an orderly manner; and the difficulty of the task of arranging
them in a lucid and convincing manner is such that several years of
exclusive devotion to that task would be requisite for its accomplishment.
        4th, that up to within a few years, new results were continu-
ally coming in in such profusion as to leave me no leisure to set
forth old ones.
        5th, that I have no natural gifts of making myself understood
and my thoughts appear to me in a garb so foreign from the ordinary
ways of thinking, that it would be a difficult matter to translate
them into the language used by readers.

[check mark]                         3.

The other was a very large work, done with much care. However,
when it was done, I found it to be written too much from its own
standpoint. It did not examine opposed opinions with sufficient
sympathy and understanding; there was an offensive tone throughout;
it was unconvincing, and utterly unworthy of the theory which it
had the honors to defend. I have since thought much and experimented
much upon how the book should be written. I can now write a treatise
which shall restrain every assertion in it within the limits in
which it shall be absolutely convincing, which shall notice every-
thing of importance that has been said on each topic, and shall meet
every issue squarely and fairly.
        Having thus explained why this work has not been written
before, I will run through a slight description of its contents.

Professor William James (one of the first living psychol-
ogists) writes: "There is no more original thinker than yourself
in our generation.  You have suggested more important things
to me than, perhaps, anyone whom I have known."
        The President of Clark University, Dr. G. Stanley Hall,
writes:  "It will be of great importance for both the philosoph-
ical work and representation in this country."
        Professor Josiah Royce, of Harvard University writes:
"What has so far appeared has been so novel, so stimulating, so
profound in many of its general conceptions, so ingenious in
its combinations, that I only express my natural appreciation
of your genius when I say that I deeply desire to see the fin-
ished product, and I am sure the enterprise deserves the sup-
port of every lover of philosophic progress in this country."
        Dr. Francis E. Abbot (author of Scientific Theism, etc.)
writes:  "I must have your volumes, because I know you to be
one of the very few Americans who have manifested a genius for
speculation, and because I want all the light I can get from
those few.  We have had parrotry in philosophy ad nauseam; and
I want your books, because I am certain to find in them the
working of a deep, acute, and powerful mind, instead of con-
tortional attitudinizing and cheap philosophical gossip."
        Professor Simon Newcomb (the leader of American Science
in his line) writes:  "I am persuaded that whatever you might
write on the subject of scientific philosophy would be provoca-
tive of thought and discussion, and therefore interesting,
whether one accepted your conclusions or not."

2 ✓

Professor O. C. Marsh (President of the National Academy
of Sciences) writes:  "I am very glad that this great work is
so far advanced toward publication."

forms of immediate inference,

        A s B,                  A s B,
(4)     ∴ B is A.               ∴ A is B.

It is obvious that (1) and (4) together will enable us to reduce {del}these{/del}
{del}forms{/del} above distorted forms of inference to Barbara. All such moods
as Cesare, Celantes, Datisi, Dabitis and Frisesomorum may be put
into these forms.

This seems to me sufficient to illustrate the advantage of the sub-
stitution of identical propositions as a method of logical analysis.

                                      C. S. Peirce

I now pass to the results of substituting identical propositions
for premisses in the various {del}[unclear: figures?]{/del} {add}moods{/add} of syllogism. This procedure
applied to the first figure gives nothing but repetitions of the
other premise in the conclusion, which shows how simple
and so to say pure from all admixture of special characters
these moods are.

It should be observed by the way that the only identical pro-
positions are,
        All X is X,
        No non-X is X,
        Some X is X,
The propositions, No X is non-X, and Some non-X is X, are for the
present purposes not to be regarded as identical ones, since they
depend on properties of the negative and the particular.

In the second figure, we have just now seen that Camestres gives
the contraposition of A into E, showing that it involves the first
property of the negative. It does not involve the second property, {add}for{/add}
it can be reduced to the first figure without conversion; thus,

        Camestres                    Reduction
        All M is P,                  No not-P is M,
        No S is P,                   All S is not-P,
        ∴ No S is M.                 ∴ No S is M.

Baroko may be reduced in the same way. On the other hand Cesare
and Festino involve the second property of negation, as is shown
by such forms as,

        No Y is X,                   No non-X is X,
        All X is X,                  All Z is X,
        ∴ No X is Y                  ∴ All Z is non-non-X.

In the third figure, Disamis and Bocardo only involve the first
property of the particular. Thus we have,

        Some X is v-X,
        All X is Y,
        ∴ Some Y is v-X.

The most philosophical way of reducing these moods to the first
figure is, therefore, as follows: -

        Disamis                         Reduction

        Some S is P,                    Any v-S is P,
        Any S is M,                     Some M is v-S,
        ∴ Some M is P.                  ∴ Some M is P.

Bocardo {del}is reduced{/del} in the same way except that not-P is substituted
for P. On the other hand, Datisi and Ferison depend upon the
second property of the particular and we have,

        All X is X,                     All X is Y,
        Some X is Y,                    Some X is v-X,
        ∴ Some Y is X.                  ∴ Some v-X is Y.

Of the {del}two{/del} moods of Theophrastus, Celantes depends on the properties
of the negative, Dabitis on those of the particular, and Fisesomorum
on the properties of both.

The {del}moods{/del}
Inferences in Dimatis, Felapton, {del}Bomalip{/del} Baralipton, Fapesmo
are resolvable into two steps.

        C. S. Peirce

Notes on Mrs. Franklin's Article Syllogism

327) I cannot admit that deductive reasoning, or any
reasoning, consists in stating explicitly anything.  (The
word explicitly is redundant, because a statement is es-
sentially an explicit, or even a formal, assertion.)  I
think reasoning is an operation of the intellect and state-
ment is an outward act.  It is true that I belong to that
school of logicians who hold that products of the understand-
ing are of the nature of signs and that logic equally applies
to mental signs and material signs of certain kinds.  But
when I speak of a proposition, rather than a judgment, as
being more general, I also speak of argument as occupying
a similar relation to reasoning.  I think those opposed
to my views in respect to logic dealing with outward signs
would say with some justice that it leads to such a monstrous
statement as that 'reasoning is stating.'

        But that is not my only objection.  I do not think
that reasoning is judging, or that an argument is a proposi-
tion.  I think reasoning is an operation whereby a judgment
is produced, - not any such operation, but a particular sort
of such operation...

328  Is it not defining the more known by the less known
to give this (Kant's) definition of Schluss.  What do we mean
by an implicit assertion?  An assertion not made.  If it is

vol 2.  [in Baldwin's Dictionary of Philosophy & Psychology, M^cMillan, 1901]
        pp. 628, 629-33

not made what is the sense of speaking of it?  I see no
answer except that though not actually made, it is deducible
from what is asserted.  This seems to me a very serious objec-
tion and is the reason why I have never used this definition,
but have always said that the conclusion of a deductive reason-
ing is believed by the reasoner to represent a fact, no analogue
of which could be otherwise as long as such premisses were true, -
either in the existing world or in any hypothetical world in
which any proposition or all would be false. . .

.3 9  You say in the most useful form of mediate reasoning
there is involved the dropping of information?  It is better to
look upon the colligation into a copulative proposition as one
step of the reasoning and the elimination as another.  One can-
not say that the former step is less useful or difficult than
the other.  Indeed, all the rules for finding a middle term about
which Aristotle expatiates are merely apparatus for colligating
premisses.
        But in the example m, information is dropped although
the same terms appear.  It is like this:
                    All men are mortal
                    Whatever lives after death has a soul
                    ∴.All men without souls are mortals who will not
                    live after death.
Therefore the dropping of information and elimination are not the
same ({del}as is stated, p.2?{/del} The above conclusion has dropped all

information about men with souls.  Here is another example:

          Some men are wicked

          Some men are fools

      .'.Some men are either wicked or fools.

Information is manifestly dropped; yet there is no elimination.

     When a middle term is eliminated, information about the

relation of the extremes is always dropped, and therefore what is

said immediately after the example (_r_) needs more precise statement.

.330  _Eliminative reasoning_ is, strictly speaking, reasoning

from a single premise.  (I should prefer to speak of inference

here.  Reasoning is too integrated.)  I do not think it is

properly definable as "saying" anything.  But it is a process of

thought the essence and purpose of which is to abstract from all

consideration of something uninteresting on that occasion.  It is

not the question whether any positive _information_ about the

eliminated term is there or not.  The point is that we want to

free our minds from an idle intruder.

          All men are mortal

          Enoch is a man

      .'.Enoch is a mortal man, if there is any such thing

          as a mortal man.

Here all the information that is dropped in the ordinary conclusion

is already dropped.  Yet so far there is no elimination.  When

we pass from this to Enoch is mortal, we have dropped some informa-

tion _about Enoch_ but no other information about _men_ (which is not

already dropped in the previous concl usion.)

You will perceive that the definition of eliminative
reasoning given errs very badly in implying that there must be
two premisses.  On the contrary, if the steps are fully separated
in the analysis, there never is but one premiss in such inference...

.34 To say that syllogism consists in stating in one proposi-
tion the truth involved in two non-relative propositions after the
elimination of a common term is rather a loose statement.  In the
first place, a syllogism is an object denoted by a concrete noun
"syllogism" and is not a noun of action.  It does not consist in
"stating" its proposition; nor in asserting that proposition
without stating it.  I really think this won't do as a matter of
logical form of statement.
        But that is the least objection to the definition.  It is
founded on the unfounded notion that elimination such as takes
place in ordinary syllogism involved two propositions, and these
non-relative.  I have shown above that there need not be two
propositions.  I will not show that the non-relativity is an ac-
cidental character arbitrarily introduced without support either in
reason or in historic usage.
        What characterizes the eliminations of ordinary syllogism,
as Boole amply showed, is that the process may be so analyzed as to
give, just before the elimination, 'X that is not X', for which
'nothing' is then substituted.  But relative inferences as often
proceed on the same principle.

Every mother loves her child.        πᵢ  πⱼ     m̅ᵢⱼ ψ lᵢⱼ)

In fact, every elimination in any kind of algebra
depends upon the fact that for some particular form of term a
general value can be substituted; although in ordinary algebra
this is masked by the peculiarity that division is there deter-
minative.  However, to stick to the matter in hand, in logic
elimination can only take place because some particular form of
term some second intentional term is predicable.

items.title: That Categorical and Hypothetical Propositions are one in essence, with some connected matters

pages.t:
[unclear: / On]                                             MS. 787    [10] 1

That Categorical and Hypothetical Propositions are one in essence,
with some connected matters.
The doctrine called "exact logic" is, as I understand it, the doctrine
that logic has to derive very essential aid from mathematics.
It thus takes for granted certain "definitions", that is to say,
* analyses of the essential nature of mathematics and of
{del}logic{/del}. I will not insist that every exact logician need conceive
the matter precisely as I do; but it is my conception alone
which I am authorized to expound.

In order to make clear to our minds precisely what mathe-
matics is, let us remember that there are men who make a
business of the practice of mathematics. A professional
mathematician is called in when new situations of extreme
complexity arise in which it would seem that known
conditions suffice to lead to determinate conclusions, but
when there is some doubt as to the precise nature of those
conclusions. Observe then the question put to the mathematician
always is, "what follows from such and such premisses?" As a part
of this phenomenon, observe, too, that the party who consults
the mathematician always begins by informing the latter con-
cerning a mass of facts; and the mathematician does
not, qua mathematician, consider himself in the least
responsible for the accuracy of any matter of fact, whatever.
This has always been the understanding at all periods of

history, except so far as mathematicians have been confounded
with astronomers or with dynamicists. Thus, the ancient ge-
ometry, besides its axioms, or κοιναὶ ἔννοιαι, "things which every
body knows," has postulates, which the student is asked to grant,
although the teacher in no way guarantees their truth. Among
{add}the postulates, for example,{/add} {del}them{/del} was that proposition {del}about{/del} {add}that{/add} two straight lines in a plane
cut by a third so as to make the sum of the internal angles on
one side less than two right angles will meet on that side if
sufficiently produced; and it {del}is a{/del} {add}was only a{/add} false test {del}which because{/del} {add}that one was at{/add}
time in vogue that converted this into an axiom. Everything
which the employe of the professional mathematician calls
a fact becomes for the mathematician himself a "hypothesis."
For as to when the facts really are, he does not concern him-
self.

The next {del}thing{/del} {add}phenomenon{/add} to be noticed is, that it is the rarest
thing in the world for the professional mathematician to answer
the precise question which is put to him. For the question put
to him is almost always too difficult for his powers. Often, too,
he sees that, as it is expressed, it does not meet the real
want. The first part of the task of the professional mathe-
matician is, therefore, to frame a hypothetical question for
himself. The pure mathematician does the same thing, only
that instead of making his hypothesis as near as possible to
alleged facts, he is more influenced by the beauties and sym-

metries, and facilities of the hypothesis itself; and instead of ma-
king his question correspond as closely as possible to practical wants,
he {del}entire{/del} {add}rather{/add} follows the line of least resistance. The hypotheses of
mathematicians are distinguished by being very definite in {del}regard{/del}
{del}to{/del} respects which make differences in the conclusions that can
be drawn, and in being perfectly indefinite in other respects.
The first task of the mathematician, that of forming (under
whatever suggestions) an arbitrary hypothesis, having been per-
formed, all that remains for him to do is to trace out the
consequences of {del}that{/del} hypothesis. In order to do this, he first
constructs a diagram, which, so far as I {del}[unclear: can?]{/del} call to mind, is
in every case either a geometrical diagram, composed of
continuous lines, or it an array of discrete {del}[illegible]{/del} objects having
certain “rules” associated with them, - such as an algebraical
formula. {del}Upon{/del} {add}Over{/add} that diagram, whether imagined or put upon
paper, the mathematician can exercise a power, which as
compared with that of a chemist or a philologist over the
objects of his study, seems almost omnipotence. To try a philo-
logical experiment a man may have to travel {del}a{/del} thousands of miles
only to encounter still greater difficulties, {del}A them{/del} {add}than to{/add} perform a
given chemical experiment may be the study of a year; and
the performance itself may occupy another. But the mathema-
tician can perform an experiment upon his diagram in
a second. {del}Naturally{/del} the natural result is, that all that element of

4

uncertainty which consists in insufficiency of experiments becomes
practically eliminated from mathematics. Everybody knows that
an error may occur in adding up a column of figures; and
what is possible once is possible always. It is, therefore, possible
that everybody has always made a mistake in adding 2 to 2.
So that, strictly speaking, that 2+2=4 is not absolutely certain.
But the experiment has been made so many times that practically
that possibility of error is far less than others, such as that the reader
had gone crazy and that it is {add}his{/add} insane delusion that anybody
ever thought 2+2=4. The point I wish to make is, that the mathe-
matician depends, like {del}over{/del} the researcher into any positive
science, upon observation; and that there is no absolute dif-
ference between the convincingness of mathematical {add}(or hypothetical){/add} and
of positive {add}(or factual){/add} observations. But only a very great
contrast due to the vastly greater {add}(though not absolute){/add} {del}difficulty of{/del} control which
the mathematician has over the objects of his study, these
being hypotheses of his own creation.
Thus, mathematics is the most abstract of all the sciences,
since it does not {del}deal{/del} assert any positive facts,-- that is, any
obstinate facts, which are as they are apart from the {del}creation{/del} {add}figments{/add}
of the mind, but only deals with {del}the{/del} facts under arbitrary
hypothetical conditions.
The positive sciences are, for various reasons, best classified
with reference to the special kinds of observations upon which

they depend. Thus, he who has a special training for microscopy, for
acoustical experiments, for hypnotism, for observing his own sensa-
tions apart from the interpretations of them, for esthetical effects,
etc. will naturally consort with other men of similar powers; and
they will produce a peculiar branch of knowledge. Among special
observations we must reckon those which are open to every {del}man{/del} {add}intelligence{/add}
of our nation, or race, or species, provided they are not open to
others; {del}[illegible]{/del} {add}although{/add} the class of minds who reason about such facts
as these will be different from that of “specialists” minds; and
{del}the character of{/del} their discoveries will {del}be{/del} have a peculiar charac-
ter.

There are, however, observations which are not only open to
all men; but which are necessarily open to all intelligences
capable of acquiring scientific {add}imperfect{/add} knowledge from observation and
reasoning. The science which is based on such observations as
these {del}is{/del} may suitably be called philosophy. This {add}science{/add} will be somewhat
narrower than {del}the ordinary conception of{/del} {add}what is commonly called{/add} philosophy, since it
will exclude ethics {del}and{/del} esthetics, etc. which repose on observa-
tions which might be foreign to some kind of scientific mind.
This science of philosophy, although it is observational, yet
{add}will have,{/add} {del}has{/del}, in a certain sense, a necessary character; for it is based
exclusively on such observations as must be open to every sci-
entific intelligence. According to my view of logic, it is im-
possible to find any absolute criterion of whether a given obser-

MS.812?  
Logic & Meth.                                                8

Let me pause here to notice {del}some{/del} {add}sundry{/add} misconceptions about
mathematical logic. I do not think that the exact logician
ought to hold that logic, or any branch of logic, however mathema-
tically treated, is strictly speaking a branch of {del}logic{/del} {add}mathe-{/add}matics. It is
like dynamics, which calls for, and has created, a special branch
of mathematics for its study; yet the dynamics, the positive
science, is one thing, and the mathematics, the purely hypo-
thetical science, is another. The fact that from a given hypothetical
premise a certain conclusion necessarily follows can only be
satisfactorily made out by mathematics; {add}for mathematics is precisely the{/add}
{del}which is the{/del} science
of hypotheses. Yet the fact that every definite, self-consistent hypo-
thesis will lead to a definite, stable conclusion, {add}instead of annulling{/add}
{del}and will not annul{/del}
and supersede itself, Hegelianally, is not a hypothetical fact, but
an ultimate, positive fact.
On the other hand, I will not admit that the mathematician
stands in any need of logic. The mathematician must reason, of
course; but he needs no theory of reasoning, because no dif-
ficulties arise in mathematics which require a theory of reasoning
for their resolution. The metaphysician does require a theory
of reasoning; because in his science such difficulties do arise.
{del}All the{/del}
{del}any{/del} special science {add}(especially the nomological sciences){/add} require, more or less, in metaphysics, and
therefore, at least indirectly, and some of them directly {add}too{/add}, require
a theory of logic. But pure mathematics can postpone such a theory.
In short, the doctrine of exact logic is, that as soon as the

elementary positive facts on which topic nexuses are made out,
it is chiefly by mathematics in its two functions of forming
hypotheses and of tracing their consequences, that logic is
to be developed. But mathematics differs from logic in not directing
its attention to these positive facts, as such, and in consequently
not asking any of those questions which characterize logic.
Prof. Schröder defines exact logic as logic studied by means
of algebra. Although {del}I should{/del} {add}while{/add} I highly appreciate the {del}mere{/del} application of
algebra to logic, yet I attach, as will be seen, a more general
meaning to the phrase "exact logic."

{del}[These present the division into speculative grammar,
logic proper,
speculative rhetoric.]{/del}

110                                                            10

Logic, in its most general sense, ought to treat of what is true of
all scientific representations, that is, of representations such as can
embody knowledge acquired by intellectual operations upon {del}sensations{/del} {add}experience.{/add}
{del}states.{/del} In a narrower sense, logic deals especially with the condi-
tions of the truth of scientific representations. But in order that
that study may be perfected, it is necessary, as a preliminary, to
consider the conditions under which a sign may be fit to embo-
dy any truth or falsity. The conception of such a science is indicated
in the title of that treatise, by Duns Scotus, Grammatica specula-
{add}sive, de modis significandi.{/add}
tiva, It is a theory of cognition,— Erkenntnisslehre,— not in pre-
cisely the same sense in which this last term is generally understood,
but a general Erkenntnisslehre, or partial Erkenntnisslehre, in
which {del}of{/del} we abstract from all that is distinctively human, and {add}from all{/add} which we
may conceive should not be true of every possible scientific intelli-
gence. As Duns Scotus perceived, the [illegible] main subject of this prelimi-
nary doctrine must be the different modi significandi.
Logic supposes that it is possible that some representations
should be satisfactory and others not so. Were this not the case, the
study would be to no purpose; and we observe that it is the case. No
student of modern science supposes that it will be possible to find
a representation which is perfectly satisfactory; the most we can
expect is that a representation shall be as satisfactory as it
pretends to be. If it is that, we call it true; if not, we call it false.
In speaking of a representation as unsatisfactory, we do not mean

that dissatisfaction is felt with it by a particular mind at a parti-
cular moment; but, regarding the search for truth as something
in which all minds join, {add}what{/add} we mean {del}that{/del} by saying that a
representation is unsatisfactory {add}is{/add} {del}we mean{/del} that the course of
inquiry is never destined to bring about, or to approximate inde-
finitely toward, a general satisfaction with the representation.
Science is a living process and not a fixed result. Consequently, it is
not sufficient to find the general conditions of the truth of a representa-
tion. It is necessary further to study the laws of the development of
scientific representations. I have named this {add}study{/add} rather fancifully, specu-
lative rhetoric, for the sake of {del}making{/del} utilizing a well-known trivium,
or triad of names of sciences, and also to {del}indicate{/del} {add}signify{/add} what it seems to me
{add}problem{/add} {add}namely{/add}
must be the main {del}study{/del} of this third logical branch, the influence of one
{del}thought upon another thought.{/del}
I may grant, at once, that all of the above is roughly stated and
vague; for this is only to say that it has those imperfections which are
inseparable from every attempt at a philosophical statement; and I
{add}shall be eager to{/add} {del}am ready to give it{/del} abandon it so soon as the better statement which
is destined to supersede it comes to my apprehension. Whatever the
reader may think of this attempt to state the problems and method
of logic, {add}in{/add} one respect he is likely to agree with me, namely {del}that{/del}
from any point of view, one of the earliest tasks of the logician
is to analyze the essential nature of assertion, and make out
what its elementary constituents are.

According to the above views, this analysis ought to depend upon ano-
ther, that of {del}inference{/del}. Inference supposes that assertions are made;
but a proximate analysis of inference is possible before we make a
minute analysis of assertion; and the former {del}will{/del} must throw a light
upon the latter. Doubt is a state of mind {del}with{/del} reference to an {add}assertion,{/add}
{add}[unclear: But not to a state of mind independently of assertion]{/add}
& connected with an instinctive desire to accept or reject it; A mind
devoid of such an impulse, - conscious or unconscious, - would not
be a scientific, or inquiring, intelligence, at all. If anybody chooses
to say that the action of this impulse postulates that the acceptance
{add}The{/add} or rejection is definitively good, or true, this, I think, may be ad-
mitted, provided not too much be implied in the word "postulates."
The {del}desire{/del} desire for a thing cannot be said, in itself, to lend the slightest
warrant to a belief that the thing exists. Moreover, the impulse is a
special tendency toward action in a special case; and can have nothing
to do with other cases. The fact that I try to get well of {del}any illness{/del} {add}each given bodily malady{/add} I
may suffer from, is no argument that I am cherishing any hope
to escape from every {del}illness which{/del} {add}malady that{/add} I may ever suffer from. So the
fact that I try to find the truth in respect to {del}any{/del} {add}each{/add} doubt that presents
itself involves no assumption on my part that there is any real
truth about all questions.

The scientific intelligence must have an impulse to put every
assertion into one or other of the classes, the {add}definitively{/add} satisfactory and the
{add}definitively{/add} unsatisfactory. Moreover, it must recognize that no arbitrary act of
his can {del}make{/del} {add}tie{/add} an assertion in one or other of these classes. For

13

inference does not begin until the mind has learned that a might

external to {del}its{/del} {add}his{/add} will is apt to bear down the most strenuous exertion

{del}to bear down the most strenuous exertion{/del} to accept or reject an

assertion. To that to which he feels he may be obliged to yield sooner

or later he hastens to yield as soon as possible. Moreover, the scienti-

fic intelligence cannot be satisfied with that which will not satisfy

{add}every such{/add} {del}any{/del} intelligence; so that it is not a private satisfaction he seeks,

but that which shall definitively satisfy the general body of inqui-

rers. Even unanimity is not enough {del}unless it{/del} {add}except so far as it{/add} is clearly perceived

that every scientific intelligence must be satisfied with the asser-

tion, or lose its scientific character. My doctrine in this respect is no apotheosis

of universal suffrage.

Thus, inference is a perception, or apparent perception, that in

view of a certain acknowledged intellectual compulsion, it will be

best, by way of yielding as soon as possible to that to which [unclear: the?] ultimate

yielding will be unavoidable, to accept a certain assertion. In particu-

lar, the simplest mode of inference,-- necessary deductive inference,--

is the perception, of or seeming perception, that, being compelled to

accept one assertion, every scientific intelligence must accept

another.

Every compulsion is something which takes place hic et nunc,

that is on a particular occasion, and affects an individual person.

It is essentially anti-general. But the {del}conception{/del} compulsion of

rational assent is not merely an individual compulsion; it is

one which it is perceived must be felt by every rational being. The

14

question how one identical compulsion can be felt by different persons
or on different occasions, whether it be pertinent or not, shall here
be passed by. This much, however, must be said, that such a general
compulsion {del}supposes{/del} a law. How a law can have a dynamic exis-
tence is perhaps rather a question of metaphysics, and a very indispens-
ible one, than a question of logic. But the perception {add}or seeming perception{/add} of a general com-
pulsion, and so of a law, must enter into every inference; so that an
inference must {add}in the inference itself; be referred{/add} {del}be conceived as belonging{/del} to a general class of infe-
rences.

To say that such and such is the fact, or the truth, is to say
that every investigating mind must, {del}[unclear: saw?]{/del} at least, be forced to

left it, was unfit to express particular propositions. Following out that idea, I
showed, in 1870, before anybody else, that we needed in logic a sign {del}[unclear: equiv]{/del}
corresponding to the sign ≦, but that that sign is unsatisfactory because
it implies that the relation is a combination of the relations expressed by <
and =, whereas in truth, as I demonstrated, it is more simple than either.
I therefore, proposed to replace the sign ≦ by <, at least in logic. The
sign I proposed has the advantage that it can be easily made in the composing
room, while the cursive form of it is rapidly written with two strokes. On account
of my priority, the sign I proposed ought, {add}in propriety{/add} to be retained, unless it be open to
very decided objections. I shall retain it. Accordingly

        h_i < d_i

means that on the occasion, i, {del}the{/del} if the idea h is definitively forced
upon the mind, then on the same occasion the idea d is definitively forced
upon the mind. On the [unclear: Philonian] view, this is the same as to say that
on the occasion, i, either the idea h is not definitively forced upon the
mind or {del}the{/del} on the same occasion the idea d is definitively forced
upon the mind. From that hypothesis, the rules of the sign < may be mathe-

48

matically deduced. I do not give them here, because by M.S. containing the
development, was many months ago borrowed by a {del}gentleman{/del} {add}friend{/add}; and I have
not yet quite lost hope of recovering it, and thus sparing myself the labor
of repeating the work. It follows from the omitted development, that while
this sign enables us, {del}ma{/del} by striking letters {add}to{/add} denote various propositions,
to express many relations, yet unless we take a letter to denote a pro-
position known or assumed to be false, it never enables us to express that
any assertion is false. There is a very good reason for adopting the
convention that

        a < b < c

shall mean a < (b < c), and not (a < b) < c. We are thus led to
inquire what {del}shall{/del} {add}must{/add} be the meaning of

        a < a < a < a < a < a < a < etc. without end.

This series of antecedents without a final consequent are seen to be equivalent
to the denial of a. Thus, without the introduction of any other sign, but merely
by the idea of an {del}infinite{/del} endless sequence, after we already have the idea of
successive sequence, we reach the idea of negation. Thus, the conceptions involved
in argumentation produce the conception of the rejection of an argumentation.
Hence, we are led to generalize our idea of argumentation, from the percep-
tion that one {del}thing{/del} {add}assertion{/add} has to be admitted because another is admitted, to em-
brace {del}that or{/del} also that process of thought {add}in{/add} by which we think that though
one assertion is true yet another is not thereby necessarily true. It is
not the {del}one{/del} primitive conception of argumentation, but this generalized
conception which covers the entire field of hypotheticals. As soon as we

have the idea of absurdity, we can conceive that a certain argument would
logically, lead to absurdity. Now an argument which can lead to absurdity
is false; and an argument which is false can in some conceivable case
lead to absurdity. Hence, as soon as we admit the idea of absurdity,
we are bound to class the rejection of an argumentation among argu-
mentations. Thus, {del}$ as was said,{/del} {add}a{/add} proposition is nothing more nor
less than an argumentation whose propositions have had their as-
sertiveness removed, just as a term is a proposition whose subjects
have had their denotative force removed.

vation is necessary, in that sense; and continual errors in this respect
in the course of the evolution of the sciences {del}will be{/del} ought to be expected.
This {add}quasi-{/add}prediction, based on the above {del}read{/del}{add}Theory{/add} of the nature of philosophy, {add}has been{/add} is
amply fulfilled in the history of philosophy; and this is a probable ar-
gument in favor of the [unclear: I] approximate correctness of that theory. But
it does not follow that there is no method by which philosophy may be
{add}gradually and{/add} indefinitely improved and {del}the{/del}{add}its{/add} errors be successively eliminated.

Whatever the philosopher thinks that every scientific intelligence
must observe, will necessarily be something which he {add}himself{/add} observes, or
seems to {del}know{/del} observe, {del}himself. In my opinion{/del} I have several times
argued, at some length, that {add}the unity of{/add} personality is in some measure illusory,
[diagram: left marginal bracket enclosing the following passage]
that our ideas are not so entirely in the grasp of an ego as we fancy
that they are, {del}and{/del} that personal identity differs rather in degree than
in kind from the unity of "public opinion" and gregarious intelligence,
{add}and that there is a sort of identity of {del}consciousness{/del} dynamic continuity in all intelligence.{/add}
Accepting this opinion, a {del}[unclear: man]{/del}{add}man{/add} is not so radically devoid of the power
of saying what every scientific intelligence must observe, if he has
the power of saying what he observes himself. If he is in dynamic
continuity with {del}himself{/del}{add}his whole self{/add}, he is in the same kind of continuity, albeit,
less intimate, with the whole range of intelligence. He can observe, in
a fallible, yet genuine, observation what it is that every scientific
intelligence must observe. Such observations will, however, require
correction; because there is a danger of mistaking special observations
about intelligences peculiarly like our own, for observations that are
open to every "scientific intelligence," by which I mean an intelligence

7

that needs to learn and can learn (provided there be anything for it
to learn) from experience. {add}[unclear: Stick]{/add} {del}[illegible]{/del} I would here define experience
as the resultant of the mental compulsions {del}of the pure without{/del} from
the course of life; and I would define learning as the gradual approxi-
mation {del}toward{/del} of representations toward a limiting definite agreement.
My theory has to be that not only can man, thus observe {del}that is{/del}
certain phenomena are open to every scientific intelligence, but that
this power inheres {del}p{/del} essentially in every scientific intelligence.
The doctrine of exact philosophy, as I understand that phrase, is,
that all danger of error in philosophy will be reduced to a minimum
by treating the problems as mathematically as possible, that is, by
constructing some sort of a diagram representing that which is
{add}supposed to be{/add}
open to the observation of every scientific intelligence, and {add}thereupon{/add} mathema-
tically,-- that is, intuitionally,-- deducing the {add}consequences{/add} {del}propositions{/del} of their hypo-
thesis. [illegible]

Philosophy, in the sense defined, will have two branches, logic
and metaphysics, resting respectively upon observed facts relating
to knowledge, or representation, and upon observed facts relating to
being.

There seems to be ample reason to think that metaphysics cannot
take a single step in any security, except under the guidance of a
developed doctrine of Logic. Logic, on the other hand, is only
weakened and led astray when {del}we{/del} {add}it{/add} attempts to {add}borrow{/add} {del}derive{/del} its principles
from metaphysics. Hence, the general order of study should be Mathematics, Logic,
Metaphysics, Special Sciences.

items.title: On the Logical Nature of the Proposition (Dicisign)

pages.t:
[unclear: Dicisign?]
1

On the Logical Nature of the {del}Proposition{/del},
By C.S. Peirce.

Mrs. Franklin's pointing out, in her lucid manner, that one
and the same proposition may receive many forms of _statement_
rejoices me, not only as a logician, but as a lover of logical English.
A _statement_ is properly, an act or the result of an act performed
with a proposition. But the word "statement" is not only, as Mrs.
Franklin says, often applied to the proposition itself, but this noun,
as well as the verb "to state", are currently, still worse abused to signify
an act or the product of an act performed with a statement (in the
proper sense). Namely, in such lingo "statement" and "state" are
synonyms of _assertion_ and _assert_. These distinctions are not
"minor points" in logic. For the question of what constitutes a
proposition (in the logical sense {del}of{/del} of the meaning of a statement) is
today perhaps the most actively disputed of all questions of logic.
Now {del}it{/del} {add}this{/add} is a problem of no little difficulty, at best; but it becomes
a great deal more so when a proposition is confounded either
with a statement, with an assertion, with a psychical act of
judging, or with an act of assent. Some German logicians
there are, notably Herbartians, who distinctly attach to the
word Urtheil the signification that English logicians (after
the scholastics) properly attach to the word "proposition";
but the majority of German logicians confound the Urtheil
in that sense with the act or product of the act signified by
the verb urtheilen. The effect of this is to blur the question of
the essence of the proposition and to render it almost

Princip
2
• insoluble.

In order to show that the distinctions mentioned do facilitate
the solution of this problem, as well as because the matter {del}may{/del} is
interesting in itself, I propose to state, as briefly as I intelligibly can,
my own opinion of the essence of the proposition. It would re-
quire too much space to set forth my reasons for adhering to it;
and still less could I here notice all the objections to my theory
which {del}that{/del} I have taken into consideration. As to the theory itself, it seems
to me quite likely that further studies will show that it is too simple;
but I believe it to be {unclear: a} rough sketch of the true state of the case.
A sign is an object {del}which{/del} capable of determining in a
mind a cognition of an object, called the object of the sign.
A sign is a species under the genus representamen. A represen-
tamen is an object, A, in such triadic relation to an object, B,
for an object, C, (the italicized prepositions merely indicating
a difference between the {del}two{/del} dyadic relation of A to B, and
that of A to C) that A is fit to {del}determine C{/del} {add}[unclear]{/add}
create C by the determination of something, so that C shall be in the
same triadic relation to A, and thereby (such is the peculiar
nature of this triadic relation) to B, for some third object, C',
determinable in the same manner, and so on ad infinitum.

Decision

X

In order to show that the distinctions mentioned facilitate
the solution of that problem, and partly as matters interesting in
itself, I will briefly state my own opinion as to the essence of
the proposition, I could not, in a limited space, set forth my
reasons for adhering to it; much less could I give the answers
to the various objections to it which I have taken into consideration.
As to the theory itself, I think it more likely than not that
further studies will show that the matter is not so simple as
I make it to be; but I believe that, as a rough sketch of the
truth, it is correct.

{add}pretend{/add} A sign is an object capable of determining in a mind an inter-
cognition of an object called the object of the sign. A sign is
a representamen; and the definition of a representamen does not
refer to a mind. A representamen is an object, A, in such triadic
relation to an object, B, {add}for{/add} an object, C, (the italicized preposi-
tions merely indicating a difference between the two relations)
that it is fit to determine C to being in the same triadic relation
to A, and thereby (owing to the peculiar nature of this triadic relation)
to B, {add}for{/add} some third object, C', determinable in the same manner,
and so on ad infinitum. [{del}In this definition I have employed the word
"relation," for the sake of its familiarity. But I have always endeavored,
as far as convenience allowed, to limit the word "relation" to a character
of a set of subjects which may all be of the same category of
being, and to I have found it advisable to confine my own studies{/del}

1. Ms. 793

For the purposes of this inquiry a Sign may be defined as
a Medium for the communication of a Form. It is not {add}logically{/add} neces-
sary that anything possessing consciousness, that is, feeling
of the peculiar common quality of all our {del}modes{/del} feeling should
be concerned. But it is necessary that there should be, two,
if not three, quasi-minds, meaning things capable of varied deter-
mination as to Forms of the kind communicated.

relation to the interpretant the sign is active, determining the

interpretant without being itself thereby affected.

The Art of Reasoning.

Chapter I. What is a Sign?

Three ways of considering things must be distin-
guished, before we can begin to answer this hard
question. Take a buttercup. We notice it is pretty,
or rather pleasing, warm, and healthy-looking. America
is a continent. The moon changes its appearance.
These are qualities*. Let us call a thing considered as
having a quality, a quale (plural: qualia.) But the
buttercup held under the chin, imparts a yellow
tinge to the {del}[illegible]{/del} skin. America is grander and freer
and fresher than {del}some other{/del} {add}the{/add} continents Europe, Asia,
and Africa. The moon's phases depend on its angular
distance from the sun. These are the relations of

* The word quality has another well-established meaning
in which it is restricted to those attributes of things which
have degrees of intensity, such as colors, smells, tastes, reso-
nance, and the like.

one thing & another. Those that the most real {del}[illegible]{/del}
are forces (in the most general sense of that word);
others, less real, are more comparative qualities, or, to
use a single word, ratios. Let us call a thing consi-
dered as one of a pair, {del}[illegible]{/del}
{del}[illegible]{/del}, a relate. {del}The relate{/del}
When the relation is a force, the relates may be
called reagents; when the relation is a ratio, the
relates may be called terms. If the yellow reflection
of the butter-cup is very {del}show{/del} intense, the children
say it reveals a love of butter {add}within them,{/add} {del}which{/del} is a weakness
if not a reproach. At least, the discovery of it
seems to elicit some jeering. There are certain
American ideas of which our continent is the sponsor.
The moon {add}signifies{/add} {del}means{/del} much to the poet and to the
astrologer. These are the significations of things.
Let us call a thing considered as having a signification,
a representament.

9
with which greater and {add}distance{/add} {del}nearer{/del} can put themselves into
physical connection. A distance cannot be described
except as the multiple of {del}a bar{/del} the length of a bar
deposited somewhere, so that people can bring away
replicas made by physical connection with it. A
direction cannot be described except by reference
to some fixed direction marked by some object, as
the centre of motion of the pole star, {add}with{/add} {del}and one which{/del}
people can put themselves into physical connection. The
difference between turning to the right or the left,
can only be known by reference to something with which
both parties can put themselves into physical connection.
Similar considerations apply to time. In short, nothing can
be identified except by signs which depend for their repre-
sentative value upon a physical connection with the
thing signified on the one hand and {del}by{/del} with the person
using the sign on the other hand. What these indices refer
to can only be ascertained by actual observation.
There are some words in all languages which fulfill
this function, but only with some assistance. Among
them may be especially mentioned, "I", "you," "this,"
"that," "who," "which." These are called pronouns,-- by an old
Latinism [unclear: proterm?], -- for instead of their standing for nouns,
it is these [unclear: ?] words which directly identify the subject
of discourse, {add}an office{/add} {del}and{/del} the nouns only imperfectly fulfill.

The word "Y" can never be understood, unless the hearer
takes the trouble actually to observe who it is that is speaking.
The word "you" cannot be understood, unless {del}he{/del} {add}one{/add} finds
reason in the attitude, countenance, etc. of the speaker
to infer that he is addressing a person who can be
seen, or indirectly observed. The words "this" and "that"
need to be accompanied by gestures to be quite unmistakable;
and these gestures it is which, being observed, are the
real indices. The words "who" and "which" refer to
names in collocation with them; and it is this observable
collocation, not the mere word, which make those names
identifiable.

I have defined an index or indication as a sign {del}by virtue
of two physical connection{/del}
{add}by virtue
of{/add} physical connection. Experiential connection would
be more explicit; for I mean by physical connection that
the sign occurs in our experience {add}in relation to the{/add} when and where {add}of{/add} the object
{del}if represented.{/del} The phrase "our experience" is significant.
Experience is the course of life, so far as one attend to it;
"Our experience", I say, because unless two persons had
some experience in common, they could not communi-
cate at all. If their experience were identical, they could
furnish one another no information. But to the experience
both have in common, the several experiences of the
two connect other occurrences; {add}and somewhat shares in a collective experience.{/add}
{del}there is{/del} an index connects {del}some{/del}
{del}thing in{/del} a new-experience with former experience.

Symbols. The word “Symbol” has already many meanings;
and I shall ask leave to add a new one. Among its
early significations, perhaps the original one, is that
of a match-rod, a somewhat arbitrarily adopted
mode or phrase, by which persons of one party recog-
nize one another. It is merely in this sense that the
church creeds are called Symbols. So a flag is a
symbol, etc. I think, then, that I shall not wrench
the word too much if I use it to mean a sign to
which a general idea is attached by virtue of a habit,
which may have been deliberately {add}instituted{/add} or may
have grown up in a natural way, {add}and perhaps{/add} have been
acquired with one’s mother’s milk, or even by {del}inheritance{/del}
{del}at birth.{/del} heredity.

These, let us honestly confess, are valid arguments; but those with
whom they weigh ought, on their side, to confess that they are not
absolutely demonstrative. For that reason, their precise value will
be better estimated after we have examined the fundamental con-
siderations which lie upon the other side of the question.
I have elsewhere expounded and {del}[unclear: sage?]{/del} defended my conception
of the nature of logic, and I cannot repeat the arguments here.
I shall only {del}repeat{/del} rehearse, in abridged form, some of the conclusions
[red arrow] p.219
Logic, in its general sense, is, as I believe I have shown, only another
name for semiotic (σημειωτική), the quasi-necessary, or formal,
doctrine of signs. By describing the doctrine as "quasi-necessary,"
or formal, I mean that we observe the characters of such signs
as we know, and from such an observation, by a process which
{del}may be{/del} I will not object to naming Abstraction, we are led to
statements, eminently fallible, and therefore in one sense by no means
necessary, as to what must be the characters of all signs used by a
"scientific" intelligence, that is to say, by an intelligence capable of
learning by experience. As to that process of abstraction, it is itself
a sort of observation. The faculty which I call abstractive observation
is one which ordinary people perfectly recognize, but {add}for{/add} which the
theories of philosophers sometimes hardly leave room. It is a
familiar {del}clear{/del} experience to {del}all men{/del} every human being to wish
for something quite beyond his present means, and to follow the
wish by the question, "Should I wish for that thing just the [unclear: terminate]

I had ample means to gratify it?" To answer that question, he
searches his heart, and in doing so, makes what I term an

A sign, or representamen, is something which stands to somebody

when a man recalls what he was thinking of at some previous

Teleological Logic

—

By C. S. Peirce

—

Begun 1865 May 14.

Chapter I

Definition

Logic is Objective symbolistic.
Symbolistic is the semiotic of symbols
Objective symbolistic is that branch of symbolis-
tic which considers relations to Objects.
Semiotic is the science of representations
Representation is anything which is or is repre-
sented to stand for another {add}and{/add} by which that other
may be stood for by something which may stand for
the representation.
Thing is that for which a representation stands
prescinded from all that can serve to establish
a relation with any possible relation.
Form is that respect in which a representation
stands for a thing, prescinded from all that can

sense as the basis of a representation & therefore
from its connection with the thing.

        Thus Science is divided into
1 Positive Science. Or the science of things.
2 Semiotic. Or the science of representations.
3 Formal Science. Or the science of forms.

        Representations

are of three kinds according to their truth or
coincidence with their objects. These are

1 Signs. Representations by virtue of a convention.
2 Symbols. Representations by virtue of original or
acquired nature.
3. Copies. Representations by virtue of a sameness
of predicates.

By a symbol is meant such a representation as
{del}will be immediately{/del} is regarded as a representation
in another system of representations. A word,
for instance upon being presented to the mind
immediately calls up a conception of the object
without resembling it and without any reference

to the convention, which has however existed.

Concepts are a species of symbols.

A symbol is [unclear: translated?] by a logos, equivalent
to another symbol in the system in which it is re-
garded as a symbol, and stands for an object.

A Logos is an embodied form.
An object is an informed thing.

The science of the general conditions {add}to{/add} which every
symbol is subjected in so far as it is related
to { a logos is General Grammar
   a language is General Rhetoric.
   an Object is General Logic.

Hence we have

[diagram: classification tree headed “Science,” branching to “Formal Science,” “Semiotic,” and “Positive Science”; under “Formal Science” appears “Science of Ethics” and “Grammar”; under “Semiotic” appears “Symbolistic” and “Rhetoric”; under “Positive Science” appears “Science of Semiosis” and “Logic.”]

In any inquiry conducted by means of logical analysis

is needful that every premise adopted should made perfectly

distinct; and with a view to this as well as for the sake of generality

the result, every clause should be removed from the premises,

which cannot alter the form of the conclusion, but can only

[unclear: its] applicability. It follows that no attention should be paid

{del}conditions of facts, or any other{/del} to unanalysable qualities

except to note that one is not another. The only point upon

2  which attention is to be centred is the form of relations

Logic ought, as far as possible, to be conducted by means of

logical analysis. Whatever {add}truth{/add} cannot be reached by logical analysis

of ordinary experience is not, properly speaking, a truth of logic.

We ought therefore to dismiss from logic every {del}[illegible]{/del} character

of the human minds which cannot be analysed into forms

of relation. In particular, we should have nothing to say in logic

about consciousness, which is an extremely vague term but

chiefly signifies the general tinge of one's waking life, about

3 which we can only say that it is probably different in different

persons, while logic must be the same for all persons. Since

we are not to speak of consciousness at all, we are not to speak ^

of thinking and other modifications of consciousness.

The most fundamental problem of logic is to form a

{del}the{/del} definition of a sign. For all logical operations can

be conducted in signs. Indeed, they cannot be conducted other-

wise, for all {add}logical{/add} mediation has the form of dialogue, and all

Thoughts, - intellectual thoughts, - {add}have strictly all the features of signs.{/add}

{del}are signs in the strictest sense.{/del}

4 {add}In answer to this it may be urged{/add}
{del}It may be objected{/del} that logic is not concerned with all
kinds of signs. For example, a command signalled from the
flagship of a squadron is hardly open to logical criticism, as a
command. Nor is a piece of concerted music subject to logic
exactly. But the reply is obvious. As long as every logical
{add}in order to understand any logical{/add}
relation is a relation of signs, it is necessary {add}to under-{/add}
stand the general structure of a sign; and if it is only a special
kind of signs that are subject to logic, we must either seek a
more extended notion of logic or else must add to our study

What was the intellectual turn and power of the superior Bactrians
of that age? I wish I had some positive data. It is not impossible that
records should be in existence which would bear upon that question.
But I can show what it probably was; namely, of a mathematical kind.
For there is reason both a priori and a posteriori for thinking that mathe-
matical genius,--which so completely marks off a small part of
mankind from the rest, even more completely than musical talent
does,-- requires that the brain should have a greater number of deeply separated
small convolutions than is found in other brains. Now such a difference
is likely to be inherited, and those whom it affects are certain to segregate
themselves from ordinary people, and to be shunned by ordinary people,
and so to make a breed. We see, in fact, that mathematics runs in families
even more than music does. Indeed, mathematicians are always capable
of being musicians unless some defect of the auditory organs prevents.
Making a breed, or tending to do so, if we find this talent strongly marked
