but by scientific students of it?  Surely, enough has been done to make
it manifest that there is such a thing as strictly scientific logic.
For instance, the doctrine of chances is nothing else.  The doctrine
of chances has been called the logic of the exact sciences; and as far
as it goes, so precisely it is.  Its immense service to science will
not be disputed by any astronomer, by any geodesist, nor probably by
any physicist.  Pearson and Galton have shown how useful it may be in
biological and psychognostic researches.  The utility of truly scien-
tific logic, then, is indisputable.  But that general logic is today
in a bad way would seem to be sufficiently shown by the fact that it is
pursued by thirteen different methods, and mostly by a confused
jumble of those methods, of which I, a very fallible person of course
but still a scientific man who has carefully weighed them pronounce but
one,-- and that one in bad odor,-- to be alone of general validity.
It [hand: circle around “is”] is, then, not desirable that an interest in pursuing logical in-
quiries in a true scientific spirit and by acknowledged scientific
methods should be aroused?  If it be so, is not the publication of
my researches, even if they contain some errors, as likely to stimu-
late such studies as anything that could be suggested?  Slight and
fragmentary as my publications have been, dealing with the less im-
portant of my results, have they not in some appreciable degree stim-
ulated the production of such work?  I point to the third volume of
Schroeder's Logik.  Look at it, or ask him, and I think you will say
that I have exercised some stimulating {del}xxxxxx{/del} agency.  Everybody admires
(nobody more than I) the beautiful presentation by Dedekind of the
```

[hand: 34.35.]

logic of number; and Dedekind, by the way, pronounces all pure mathemat-
ics to be a branch of logic.  Read his "Was sind und was sollen die
Zahlen", and then read my paper on the Logic of Number, published
six years earlier, and sent to Dedekind, and ask yourselves whether
there is anything in the former of which there is not a plain indica-
tion in the latter.  Let me not be misunderstood.  I am simply arguing
that my papers have stimulated the science of logic.  I wish with
all my heart that the Executive Committee could have in view some other
student of logic of vastly greater powers than mine.  But even if
they had, considering how much energy has been spent in obtaining my
results, would it not be a pity not to have them presented to the
world?

        It is my belief that science is approaching a critical
point in which the influence of a truly scientific logic will be ex-
ceptionally desirable.  Science, as the outlook seems to me, is com-
ing to something not unlike the age of puberty.  Its old and purely
materialistic conceptions will no longer suffice; while yet the great
danger involved in the admission of any others, ineluctable as such ad-
mission is, is manifest enough.  The influence of the conceptions of
methodeutic will at that moment be decisive.

        Vast, however, as the utility of logic will be in that direc-
tion, provided that logic shall at the critical moment have developed
into that true science which it is surely destined some day to become,
yet the pure theoretical value of it is greater yet.  No doubt, it is
possible, while acknowledging, as one must, that logic produces useful
truths, to take the ground that it is a composite of odds and ends,

ESTIMATE OF THE LABOR REQUIRED FOR THE WORK.                    35.

a crazy-quilt of shreds and patches, of no scientific value in itself.

But seeing that pure mathematics is so close to logic, that eminent
mathematicians class it as a branch of logic, it is hard to see how
one can deny pure scientific worth to logic and yet accord such worth
to pure mathematics.  Probably there are naturalists of culture so
narrow that they would deny absolute scientific value to pure mathemat-
ics.  I do not believe the Committee will embrace such views.  And
then, there is Metaphysics to be considered.  Everybody must have his
Weltanschauung.  It certainly influences science in no small measure.
But metaphysics depends on logic, not merely as any science may occa-
sionally need to appeal to a logical doctrine, but, according to the
greatest metaphysicians, the very conceptions of metaphysics are bor-
rowed from the analyses of logic.  Now if there is any such thing as
pure scientific value, as distinguished from the administration one
might have for a newly discovered dye, in what can it consist if not in
intellectual relations between truths?  If so it be, then, in view
of the relations of logic to metaphysics, and that of metaphysics to all
science, how can it be said that logic is devoid of scientific value,
if there be any such thing as scientific value?  If logic is the sci-
ence which my memoirs go to show that it is, it is the very key stone
in the arch of scientific truth.

        Little known as my papers have been, I believe that there
are some men whose judgments must command respect in the world of sci-
ence, who will testify to the utility of the work that I have done,
and to the probably utility of that which I am about to do.

ESTIMATE OF THE LABOR REQUIRED FOR THE WORK.                 37.

My results in each of the three do zen topics have to be
carefully revised, though for the most part that has often been done
already, have to be set into logical order, and have to be presented in
the fully convincing forms which they merit.  It is also most desir-
able that the presentation of each should be as brief {del}[illegible]{/del} and
as closely confined to what is pertinent as is consistent with com-
pleteness and with perspicuity.  A certain amount of labor must be
bestowed upon their literary polish; for my purpose requires that they
should be read by persons who are not professional logicians.  Indeed,
for persons who are disposed to think, I believe that as far as in me
lies I should make them even attractive; although I am painfully cons
[hand: ous]
science of my small literary ability.

Taking all these things into consideration, my experience
of what I can do suffices to enable me to say that six memoirs a year
is all I ought to promise, although I should confidently hope to finish
the three dozen in five years.

I should be loath to inflict as many as a million words
on a student: it would so narrow my field of influence.  I am sure
my results could not be presented as they merit, in all their convinc-
ingness in half a million.  The majority of the memoirs could be com-
pressed into about 20,000 words each; but only by laborious and clever
condensation.  A very few which might be much shorter are overbalanced
by quite as many or more that must inevitably mount to 50,000 words
each, dividing themselves advantageously into two parts.  To bring the
total within the million, seeing that they so increase in matter as the

37.

series advances that every one of the last quarter of the series is [unclear: prove]
excessively dense in matter, is going to be a task calling for all my
vigor but most needful.

        Persons whose business it is to write, and who are not trou-
bled with having too much to say, may argue that 200,000 words a
year is only 700 words a day for six days in every week, and that such
a limit can only be set by indolence.  To this I can only reply that
it would be much easier to make the memoirs three times as long as I
propose.  At that rate they would be better, taken singly.  But the
whole would be too much.  If anybody suspects me of indolence, I
shall only have to turn in all the papers; and it will be seen that I
have in each case written from three to five times as much as I in-
clude in my final copy.

                    ESTIMATE OF OTHER EXPENSES INVOLVED.

        These other expenses are mainly books, although the person
who examines and reports upon the memoirs should be remunerated for his
labor.  Historical statements and critical examinations form an essen-
tial part of the plan.  Books must be at hand.  My entire library con-
tains only about 2000 books.  I shall require 500 more costing say
$2000.

                    NEED OF THE AID ASKED FOR.

        I am bound to confess should the Carnegie Institution re-
fuse all cooperation, I should {del}h{/del}continue to be animated by a robust
faith that somehow my results would be given to the world; and I am

38.

fully satisfied that that faith is logically justified.  It might prove
mistaken; and if it did my concern would be limited to knowing that I
had performed my part.  But while I fully believe that I shall succeed
in any case, I have no definite {add}[hand: idea]{/add} of how I could do so in default of the
aid which I ask from the Carnegie Institution; and in that sense I can
truly say that such aid seems to be indispensable.  I believe the Ex-
ecutive Committee will help me.

        SUGGESTED PLAN FOR THE REQUISITE AID.

        I should suggest that each memoir, as finished, should be
sent by me, in MS. or typewritten, to the office of the Carnegie In-
stitution and should be at once placed in the hands of a man of my own
rank as a thinker, or higher, whose duty it should be, not to go into
any criticism of it but to look it over,- say in an hour or two,- and
report upon whether or not it seems to be such a solid piece of work
as is worthy of acceptance.  Upon his favorable report, say within a
week, the Carnegie Institution should cause a sum of money to be re-
mitted to me and should become the owner of the copyright in the memoir
sent in.

        I should suggest that if the length of the memoir was from
15,000 to 30,000 it should count as a unit, if more as two {del}[illegible]{/del}
units, and that the remittance should be so much per unit.  This is
a mere suggestion as, indeed, is the whole plan.

        The Committee might see fit to put a limit upon the number
of units that would be receivable in one year.  I do not think that
under any circumstances it could {add}[hand: exceed]{/add} nine, and that number could only be

as lies in my power, in case the Committee should require assurances of

39.

reached some year owing to special circumstances.

        The memoirs should be handed in in their regular serial
order.

        Since the books needed would be needed at the outset, if
the Carnegie Institution would supply me with 500 books of my choice
to be kept for a term of years, I would agree that my whole library
should go at my death to the free school of logic I desire to found
or to any other party whom the Carnegie Institution might designate.
If this plan is not agreeable, I should ask in some form to receive ex-
tra help the first year.  By making selections of subjects, I could
write nine memoirs in the first year; but it would be a bad plan.
The memoirs ought to be written in the order of consecution.

                PROBABILITY OF THE COMPLETION OF THE WORK.

        Each memoir is complete in itself.  The science of logic
will be completed not earlier than the sciences of biologyand of
history are complete.  But it is eminently desirable that the series
of three dozen memoirs should be completed.  Having all my life long
sacrificed every interest to logic, it might seem that I was insulting
the Executive Committee if I were to suppose their knowledge of human
nature was such that they could doubt my finishing this series if
death, total incapacity, or the necessities of daily life did not inter-
vene.  It has been represented to me, however, by persons of the high-
est credit, that the Committee would insist {del}that{/del} on some assurance that
the whole would be finished.  Without permitting myself to believe
or disbelieve this, I think I am justified in offering such assurance
as lies in my power, in case the Committee should require something of [hand: the rest.]

40.

I am in excellent health and capital trim for this work.
I do not think there will be much danger {del}of my{/del} breaking down in five
years.  However, if the Committee thinks there is, I should suggest
that in the first six months, instead of writing the first three
memoirs, I write, in six equal monthly parts, each of not less than
15,000 words, abstracts of the memoirs, six memoirs in regular order
being treated in each part.  Then in case the work of writing the
memoirs (which under this arrangement would only begin at the end of
six months), were broken off, otherwise than by the action of the Car-
negie Institution, the copyright of this abstract should pass to the
Institution; but the Carnegie Institution during those first six months
should contribute liberally to aid the production of these abstracts.
I say "liberally" because books would have to be procured.  If, on the
other hand, the series of memoirs were completed, then, but not before,
I should be at liberty to do what I pleased with the abstracts.  What
I should be pleased to {del}[unclear: endeavour]{/del} attempt would be to make out of them
a logic for the people, a charming classic for the twentieth century,
thus as a secondary object, sparing my old age the mortifications of
extreme poverty, although I am not capable of making such an object a
leading one. *  If the memoirs were, say, half of them published,
then this abstract (which I should have been continually polishing)
could be used to complete the publication.
        But abbetter plan, I think, would be to devote the first
three months to writing fuller abstracts of the last nine memoirs,
omitting altogether the historical and critical parts.  This would

be a very great loss; because though the plan might result in a toler-

            As an additional or alternative security, I should suggest,
supposing that other security were desired by the Executive Committee,
that a contract be executed between the Carnegie Institution and me by
which I should be bound to send in the memoirs with no interval between
any successive two exceeding three months, unless some visitation of
providence (say, a five month's illness, a conflagration, or a domestic

Otherwise, I failing in this, should be bound to repay to the Carnegie
Institution all money up to that time paid to me, while losing the
copyrights of the printed memoirs.  I would yearly furnish bonds that
such money should be refunded, if failure should occur within one year.
I should secure my bondsman by putting into his hands first draughts
of the memoirs for the ensuing year, which though they would not be sat-

ment.  Of course this would be but partial security.
            I beg to say lest the Executive Committee should deem this
proposition ridiculous, that I express no opinion about it.  I stand
ready to carry it out, if desired.  I am most anxious to meet what
highly creditable people believe to be the wish of members of the Com-

42.

mittee;  and no better plan occurs to me.

        Of course, in case any contract were made, the Carnegie In-
stitution would by its terms become bound to persist in the arrangement
to the end, and to publish each memoir within, say, one year of the date
of its approval.

        I have a reputation of not finishing things.  I suppose there
is some basis of truth beneath it, but it has been like every evil
reputation, exaggerated out of all semblance of truth by calumny.
It should be remembered that I was connected for a long time with the
Coast Survey; and it will be easy for members of the Committee to as-
certain that that office has been,at times, a veritable hotbed of in-
trigue, and that I, in partifular, have sometimes suffered great in-
justice there.  Voluminous memoirs were prepared by me for publication
which I never could get printed; and then I was accused, vaguely and in
intangible forms, of not getting my work ready for publication.  For
the truth of this (except that the accusations were made) I stand re-
sponsible.  I have often made this statement.  If it is not true, why
amI not called upon to go ahead with the printing?

        Excepting in the case of one early paper on the Logic of
Mathematics, which I concluded I did not know enough about to continue,
I have never had a disinclination to go on with any series of publica-
tions which I had begun.  On the contrary, the disinclination has al-
ways been on the side of those who were to pay for the printing.  When
such disinclination was manifested, of course I ceased to press the
matter.

43.

PROBABLE NET COST.

        Mills' Logic went through nine editions before the copyright
expired.  I should not expect anything like that.  But still, the
utility of these memoirs will require me to make them as agreeable read-
ing and as little tedious as their scientific character will allow.
Great pains will be bestowed upon this; and it will be perfectly proper
that they be handed over to a publisher and sold like any books.  In
time there will be some sale for them.  It would certainly make up in
considerable part for the remittances made to me.  For five or six
years' support of me and my wife, the Carnegie Institution would receive
the fruit of over forty years' meditation and labor.  For the price
of 500 books, it would, after a term of years, have 2500 books to dis-
pose of.  I think its objects would profit by the transaction.

BASIS OF MY CLAIM.

        A man has put nearly fifty years of single-minded endeavor
into a work of benefit to science.  He has a sort of claim,-vague only
in being addressed to no particular party, that he should be rewarded
for what he has done.  But the only reward which would be a reward
would be that of being enabled to complete his life-work.

        At this juncture one of the most extraordinary figures of
all humanity puts down an enormous sum of money and expresses the wish
that it be used, as the second of six emphasized aims,-
        "to discover the exceptional man in every department of
study whenever and wherever found inside or outside of schools, and

[hand: 44.]

be enabled to complete it, in the manner in which it is susceptible,
and enable him to make the work for which he seems specially designed
his life work."

        Composed as your body is, reason alone will determine your
decision.  Logic is a "department of study".  Whether or not, in this
narrow field, I am and "exceptional man",- and to be such is anything
but a good fortune, in such a direction nothing but a burden,- you will
determine; looking probably into the third volume of Schroeder's
Logik where my work is mentioned in some two hundred places.  On page
1, I am called the "Hauptförderer" of "eine grossartige Disziplin",
the "logik der Beziehungen".  Although my explanations attached to
the above list of proposed memoirs are of such a nature as to preclude
their showing how greatly the logic of relatives really determines all
my conclusions upon every topic of logic, nevertheless {del}an{/del} the impression
which a reading of those explanations would create that the subject
of relations does not constitute any overwhelming part of the subjects
of my researches is quite correct.  Should it seem to you to be true
that the duties of an "exceptional man" in the department of logic
have to be borne by me, then it will be come one of your duties to aid
me in the performance of mine to make the work for which this man
"seems specially designed his life work."  I am frank to say that
the idea that phrase embodies has long impressed me; namely, that men
seem to be specially designed for various kinds of work, and that, if
it be so, the work for which I seem to have been designed is that of
working out the truths of logic.

        If you should be led to this opinion, then my claim to the
reward for the life I have so far put into this work, the reward of be-

```
                                                                    45.

ing enabled to complete it, in the sense in which it is susceptible
of completion, is no longer so vague; but I shall then find in you a
definite party upon whom I have that claim; since in satisfying it,
you will only be carrying out one of the responsibilities which you have
accepted.
                 Whatever action you may take, it is my duty to believe,
and I do believe, that the work will get done.  At any rate, all that
I feel much concern about is that I should do my very utmost to carry
out my part effectively.  I have no disposition to even ask myself what
specifically your duty is, of which you are the sole judges, except
so far as we shall all have to render account hereafter.  Submitting,
then, my application to your kindly wisdom, I remain, gentlemen,

4 Kirkland Place, Cambridge, Mass., 23 January, 1902.

My dear President Gilman:

        It has occurred to me, and has been independently suggested to me by
others, that my brother Charles S. Peirce's work in logic is an excellent example
of the kind of work that should receive encouragement and aid from the Trustees
of the new Carnegie Institution.  He has made it the leading labor of his life
for forty years, always coming back to it as the stress of less unremunerative
occupation would permit, and bringing to bear upon it the energies of what all
recognize as a very able mind, and of very profound and arduous studies.  From
the dim vision which I have been able to get of the peculiarities of his system,
I am convinced that it is of real importance to human thought that that system
be laid before the learned world in all its fullness, and Charles himself is
deeply concerned to bring it to the definite shape of a book, in the few years
that remain to him for work.  Of course, however, it would be absolutely without
remuneration or expectation of it, and would absorb his whole time for several
years.  His friends have, for several years, done their best to get him work as
a teacher or as a scientific investigator.  I need not enlarge on the personal
obstacles to this endeavor.  His character and temper are harshly judged by many,
and have received, as I believe, far less than justice, though I am aware that
he has often stood in his own light, and sometime through a quixotic loyalty
to others.  But this is a question of intellectual power, and his intellectual
power has never, I think, been questioned.  I shall be greatly obliged to you
for any interest you are prompted to feel in this matter, and for any suggestions
you are able to make to me.

                        Yours very sincerely,

                                        James Mills Peirce.

8 Kirkland Place, Cambridge, Mass., 25 January, 1902.

My dear President Gilman:

It has occurred to me, and has been independently suggested to me by
others, that my brother Charles S. Peirce's work in logic is an excellent example
of the kind of work that should receive encouragement and aid from the Trustees
of the new Carnegie Institution.  He has made it the leading labor of his life
for forty years, always caring best to be as the slave of some unremunerative
occupation would permit, and bringing to bear upon it the energies of what all
recognize as a very able mind, and of very profound and extreme thinker.  From
the dim vision which I have been able to get of the peculiarities of his system,
I am convinced that it is of real importance to human thought that that system
be laid before the learned world in all its fullness, and Charles himself is
keenly concerned to bring it to the definite shape of a book, in the few years
that remain to him for work.  Of course, however, it would be absolutely without
remuneration or expectation of it, and would absorb his whole time for several
years.  His friends have, for several years, done their best to get him work as
a teacher or as a scientific investigator.  I need not enlarge on the personal
obstacles to this endeavor.  His character and temper are hardly judged by many,
and have worsened, as I believe, for long from justice, though I am aware that
he has often stood in his own light, and sometimes through a certain loyalty
to others.  But this is a question of intellectual power, and his intellectual
power has never, I think, been questioned.  I shall be greatly obliged to you
for any interest you are prepared to feel in this matter, and for any suggestions
you are able to make to me.

Yours very sincerely,

James Mills Peirce.

                    63 State Street, Boston,
                         Jan. 1, 1903.

          President D.C. Gilman,

               My dear Mr. Gilman,-

                              I learn that the Carnegie Insti-
tute may consider the question of the extending some support
to the publication of Mr. Charles S. Peirce's book on logic,
and I take the liberty of writing to express my sense of
the value which that book must have.

          I have had the advantage of studying philosophy with
Mr. Peirce, and while that was many years ago it gave me the
opportunity of knowing the unusual quality of his mind and
the highly original character of his contributions to studies
of that kind.

          I am convinced that it is important that he should be
enabled to put his work into a final and systematic form,and
as such a book cannot be expected to have a sale sufficient
to meet its cost, it may be impossible to secure it unless
aid be given in some form.

          If the Institute can find it within the scope of its
work to promote this branch of philosophical study, it could
hardly find a better means,in my opinion, than the encour -
agement of this book.

          I am, with great regard,   Very sincerely yours,

                              Joseph B. Warner,

To
     President D.C. Gilman,
          Carnegie Institute,
               Washington, D.C.

Now, of course, the larger the volume -- always supposing it is within

reason -- of these manuscripts, the better; for the larger would necessa-

rily be the honorarium.  I would propose now that you send me at once

anything else you may have which would do for such purpose.  You spoke in

your last letter of some other manuscript that you could have ready in

a very short time.  I want to make a list of these to accompany my letter

to the Committee, and I should like to have it as imposing as possible.

But it ought to be work that is really ready.  This manuscript could either

consist of separate and detached memoirs or complete books of the recently

written work you spoke of consisting of 150,000 words or more, or of first

installments of the series of memoirs which you proposed to publish, or

of all three.  Only put in my hands without loss of time anything you

have ready which you would feel worthy of yourself.  You understand my

project is to secure for you an honorarium; but with the condition from

the Carnegie Institution that they shall carry you along and enable you

-3-

to devote yourself to your Magnum Opus, under the direction,-- so far as

the protection of the interests of the Carnegie Institution is concerned,--

of the Committee suggested.  This seems to me to be a business plan, and

it shall go hard but I will carry it through,-- and that as soon as possible .

But keep up heart if there are delays, and feel confident that your affairs

are in hands which are not going to let them drop, and in the care of a

person who having taken this matter up will carry it through to the bitter

end,-- even if I finally have to appeal to Carnegie himself.  On the other

hand, don't write to anyone but me,-- or to Jim on the subject.  let me

deal with these people as I know how, until such time as I tell you I can

go no further.  Above all, let me have more manuscripts as soon as you can,

-- the sooner the better.  I want it early next week if I possibly can

have it.

                      Your affectionate brother,

My dear Sir,
                         I am informed that an appeal has been made to the Trustees of
the Carnegie Fund to further in some way the publication in systematic form
of the results of the work of Mr. Charles S. Peirce in Logic and Philosophy.
                         I cannot venture to make a suggestion to the Committee, with whose
plans I am, of course, unacquainted.  But I feel that it is right that I should
express my conviction of the high value of Mr. Peirce's work, and my hope that
it may be possible to give him the desired assistance.  I am sure that it would
meet with the approval of teachers of philosophy all over the country.
                         If my expression of opinion can be of any service at all in the matter,
you are welcome to use it as you see fit.

                         Believe me,
                                   Very truly yours,
                                             Geo. S. Fullerton.


Jan. 5, 1904.

                           Washington, D.C.

                                January 4th. 1905.

Dear Mr. Gilman:

              I venture to write you to express my interest in
Charles Peirce and the hope that you will be willing to give your decisive
influence to granting an appropriation from the Carnegie to enable him
to complete his work on Logic.
              I know of course the infirmities of temper which have stood in
his way in life, but I also think he is a man of genius, an opinion con-
firmed by many better judges than I, like Professor James and Professor
Royce.  I am satisfied that his work would be an important contribution
to human thought and an honor to the country.  Such a book could not be
published without aid, and it seems to me that it is distinctly of the
kind which the Carnegie Institution exists to support.  I feel a deep in-
terest in this and sincerely hope that you will bring it about.
              With kind regards,

                          Sincerely yours,

                              (Signed)  H.C. Lodge.

terest in this and sincerely hope that you will bring it about.

          Supreme Court of the United States, Washington, D.C.

                         January 5, 1903.

Dear Sir:

          My friend Dr. James has asked me to write to you upon the
question of possible aid to Mr. Charles S. Peirce from the Carnegie fund,
and I have decided to do so after inquiry and reflection.  I know Mr.
Peirce's philosophy mainly as second hand from echoes in the books of
James and Royce -- but from those and from what I have seen of Mr.Peirce
in former years, I find no difficulty in believing his contributions to
be of much importance.  If the result of keeping him along should be the
completion of his magnum opus, I should expect it to be an event of mag-
nitude, and possibly of the first magnitude.  The matter lies in my mind
in this way -- and it seems to me entirely possible that this aid might
give the world a work of genius -- that the uncertainties are less in the
extraordinary gifts and acquirements of the man than in other circumstan-
ces about which I am not well informed, and as to which you know probably
better than I.  Of the present state of progress of his book (Logic) I am
not informed.

             With much respect,

                         Very truly yours,

                                   (signed) O.W. Holmes.


President Gilman.

Princeton, N.J.  January 3, 1903.


Hon. H.D. Peirce,

        Department of State,  Washington, D.C.


My dear Sir:

        I am informed that an application to the Carnegie Institu-
tion has been made in behalf of the publication of the life-long studies
of your brother Charles.  As one who was closely associated with him in
the study of logic and philosophy for two years at the Johns Hopkins Uni-
versity, it gives me great pleasure to add my little testimony to the val-
ue of his work.  His writings in logic and philosophy have been an inspira-
tion to many young scholars, and he is acknowledged in this and other
countries as one of the first authorities in the department of logic, and
to be a man of unusual intellectual power.  I feel very sure that should
the Trustees of the Carnegie Institution undertake this publication, they
will reap their reward in the consciousness of having aided the most im-
portant of your brother's many valuable contributions to philosophy.

        Yours very sincerely,

                Allan Marquand.

HARVARD UNIVERSITY.

Committee of the Carnegie Institute,
Gentlemen:

        I have just learned with extreme regret that the appli-
cation of Dr. Charles Saunders Peirce for assistance by the In-
stitute in the execution of his plan of philosophic work has
been rejected.

        I beg to submit an urgent recommendation for a revision
of the judgment in this case.  Some few years ago, in common with
many others, I looked forward to the completion of a series of
works outlined by Dr. Peirce which he proposed to publish by
subscription, and which from one of his remarkable attainments
in logic and philosophy promised to be amongst the most notable
of American productions in these fields, and also much the largest
single systematic achievement by any American.  The plan failed
from want of support -- in my judgment, to the very considerable
loss of philosophy.  The learning, subtlety, originality and bold-
ness of his suggestions in fundamental philosophy place him among
the foremost thinkers in this country in his chosen subjects.
His work, in the Algebra of logic, has been treated with great
consideration by important German writers.  I am convinced that
a highly valuable talent will be passed by and a great scientific
work discouraged, if all support should be withheld from his un-
dertaking.

        Very respectfully yours,
             Dickinson S. Miller.

                                        January 8, 1903.

President Gilman:

     Dear Sir,
               May I express my most earnest wish that the decision of

the Institute against Mr. Charles Sanders Peirce be reconsidered ?

It seems to me, as to all of my friends who know Mr. Peirce's work, that

it will be a great opportunity lost for the advancement of the science of

logic.  Surely Mr. Peirce has done more than any other American logician

to advance the higher branches of the science.  Trusting that this and

other appeals which I feel sure will be made for reconsideration may meet

with a favorable issue, I remain,

                         Respectfully yours,

                              (signed)  Wilmon Henry Sheldon, Ph.D.

                    Dept. of Philos., Columbia University, N.Y.

        The Century Association
        7 West Forty-third Street.

To the Directors of the Carnegie Institute,
    Gentlemen:
        I am advised that Mr. Charles S. Peirce
has asked the assistance of the Carnegie Institute to en-
able him to complete his "Logic".
        It appears to me that Mr. Peirce's work could not
fail to be of very great value to all students of funda-
mental problems.
        He has thus far been able to give to the world only
parts of this work; but these have sufficed to convince
many, of whom I am one, that it would be an irreparable
loss to science should he be unable to complete it.
        In my opinion it would be an honor to the Institute
to have enabled Mr. Peirce to complete his labors; and I
sincerely trust that you will find it possible to grant
him such aid as he asks.
            I remain, respectfully,
                Henry Rutgers Marshall.

2nd Jany. 1903.

[hand: Marshall is well-known throughout the philosophical
world for his faithful accurate & well-informed work upon
aesthetics.]

Museum of Fine Arts, Boston, Mass.

                                      January 1, 1903.

My dear Sir:

                         I learn that friends of your brother, Mr.
          Charles S. Peirce, have brought before the Trustees of the
          Carnegie Institution the question of aiding in the publica-
          tion of the results of his philosophical studies.  The
          question will, I hope, receive careful consideration and
          a favorable answer from the Trustees.  I know of no oppor-
          tunity for the encouragement of research likely to be more
          wide-reaching in its ultimate influence than this; none more
          timely, the state of philosophical opinion being what it is;
          and none to which from its nature aid is more essential.
          An intellect more penetrating and powerful than your broth-
          er's it has not been my fortune to encounter, nor to hear of
          among the living; and it is a mind that for a lifetime has
          followed its own chosen and lofty paths without deviation.
          Mr. Peirce's many studies upon ultimate truths and upon the
          conditions of the search after truth have been written upon
          occasion, and lie scattered in various periodicals.  Were
          their real importance generally understood, all those who
          have at heart the higher interests of the country and the
          race would wish to be assured of their publication as a body
          of doctrine.  I sincerely trust that through the aid of the
          Carnegie Institution Mr. Peirce may have freedom to crown
          his labors by the connected expression of their results.

                    I beg to remain, very sincerely yours,

                                      Benj. Ives Gilman,

Hon. Herbert H.D. Peirce,
        Department of State.

                                            Jan. 1, 1903.


Dear Prof. James:

                         The work of Mr.Chas. S. Peirce, so far
as I have known  it hitherto, is of the kind that benefits
not only the student of philosophy but all who work with the
tools of science, for his logic is a shaping and sharpening
of the tools which all of us use when we attack the problems
nature presents to us.
        Any help that he might receive in continuing such work
as he brought before us in his lecture at Cambridge, a few
years ago, would bring in a return of value to everybody who
investigates anything thoroughly.
                         Yours sincerely,

                    Harvard College Observatory
                       Cambridge, Mass.

                                  January 1, 1903.

My dear Sir:

            Information has reached me that Mr. Charles
S. Peirce has made an application to the Carnegie Institu-
tion for aid in publishing a work on Logic.  I have known
Mr. Peirce for more than forty years, and have the highest
opinion of his intellectual capacity.  He prepared Volume IX
of the Annals of this Observatory, a work showing great
originality and careful thought.  There is no doubt that he

                 Harvard College Observatory
                    Cambridge, Mass.

                                             January 1, 1903.

My dear Sir:

                 Information has reached me that Mr. Charles
S. Peirce has made an application to the Carnegie Institu-
tion for aid in publishing a work on Logic.  I have known
Mr. Peirce for more than forty years, and have the highest
opinion of his intellectual capacity.  He prepared Volume IX
of the Annals of this Observatory, a work showing great
originality and careful thought.  There is no doubt that he
has one of the most remarkable minds in the country.  While
my knowledge of the subject of his proposed work is limited,
I am of the opinion that his application should receive
favorable consideration by the Carnegie Institution, if
conditions can be made which will insure publication at
an early date.

                         Very truly yours,

                              Edward C. Pickering.

Dear Sir:

          Your note of December 19th. to the Century Co. has been re-
ferred to me.

          The work done by Professor Charles S. Peirce upon the Century
Dictionary was large in amount and of very high quality.  It is a pleasure
for me to express my appreciation of it.  With regard to the matter of
promptness, you will understand that in work like that on the Century
Dictionary it is not easy to get everything done on schedule time and that
I found it necessary to "prod" all the members of the editorial staff more
or less.  My recollection is, however, that Professor Peirce caused no
delay on the work as a whole and that in general the material which he
agreed to deliver was delivered as it was needed.  If I should make an
agreement with him for other work I should expect him to keep it faithfully

CARNEGIE INSTITUTION
1439 K STREET
WASHINGTON, D. C.

July 30 1902

My dear Mr. Peirce:-

On behalf of the Carnegie Institution I
hasten to acknowledge receipt of your very interesting
application for aid in harvesting for coming generations
the fruit of years of reflection. I have read your long
letter, every word of it, some of it twice, and I assure
you, with the keenest satisfaction. Like you, however, I
can only guess at the attitude of the Executive Committee,
but, as for me, I say by all means, support this application.
How to do it best, may produce diverse views. It had
occurred to me that a good way would be to pay you
a comfortable salary, to live in Washington near the
great libraries, to work at the Institution and to enter
upon the printing one by one under your own eye of these
memoirs. A stenographer friend of mine has a favorite
phrase "Try it on the dog first." He has often been the dog
on whom his employing lawyers have tried their briefs.
And if you begin the work here may I volunteer my
services? Try your logic on me.

Mr Walcott, Secretary of the Ex. Com. is in Woods Hole, Mass.
and expected here on Sunday next. He will direct the disposition
of your letter. The Ex. Com. are likely to meet in August and
the Trustees in November. Dr Gilman is expected to return
from Europe about the end of September. I was very sorry to
miss your visit when you did me the honor to call.

My compliments to Madame P—— and believe
me

Cordially Yours
Marcus Baker

CARNEGIE INSTITUTION
1439 K STREET
WASHINGTON, D. C.

August 13 1902

My dear Mr Peirce:-

I greatly enjoyed your interesting letter of
the 1st which came duly and {add}now{/add} write to tell you so; and also
to tell you, furthermore, that we have had 7 copies made
of your application and proposed plan. These have been
mailed to the widely scattered Executive Committee of
whom two, Gilman and Secy. of War Root are in Europe.
Gilman is "booked" to sail for America Sept 17 so it
now seems unlikely that th Ex Com. will {del}not{/del} meet
till about the last of Sept. or early in October. The Trustees
are to meet in November. It is along about that
time, therefore, that I look for some action on Logic.

Meanwhile let me thank you for the compliment you
pay me of inviting my reading and criticism of some
of your writing. I dare not promise much and yet I shall
be delighted to do what I can in this line.

Many things have happened since the Etruscan day of 1879. The
dear little Mrs B- whom you knew, after several years of impaired
health met a crisis in 1895, when a clouded mind, and delirium
prevailed - after a Doctor's conference, Bright's Disease. With this
new information and resulting changed treatment came some
improvement but the disease was not stayed or even checked. The
end came in 1897. I have married again, have a little daughter
8 months old, and a very happy home where, we shall be glad
some day to welcome our distant cousin C. S. Peirce and his
good wife

Cordially Yours
Marcus Baker

My dear Mr Peirce:-

I would gladly read and discuss your
manuscripts with you but I dare not promise to do so for
every day and some nights are filled with duties that must
be done. That is why I wish you were comfortably housed
right here with books galore at your command.
Have you not certain chapters or books or parts of your
great work ready for the printer or at least ready for final
revision? If you have and send it {add}or them{/add} to me I will store it, in
the safe (which has been ordered) when we get it and look it at
it when I can.
And meanwhile pray accept the cordial greetings
of one who {add}would{/add} very gladly stand as midwife and god father to
your great brain child

Heartily Yours

Marcus Baker

Copy.

Dr. D.C. Gilman,
        President Carnegie Institute,
             Washington, D.C.

Dear Sir,
        I am informed that the Carnegie Institution is considering
the desirability of publishing some of the works of Mr. C.S. Peirce.

Mr. Peirce is in my opinion the deepest and most original thinker in
America.  In philosophy and particularly in logic his work has been of
vital importance.  In spite of the fragmentary nature of most of his pub-
lished writings, Mr. Peirce has profoundly influenced the thought of such
men as Professors James and Royce in America; Schiller in England and
Schroeder in Germany.  His role among contemporary philosophers has been
that of kingmaker rather than king, and his reputation with the philoso-
phic public is but a feeble index of the power he possesses and of the
part he has had in moulding the philosophy of the day.

        In Logic he is the recognized leader in America of those who fol-
low Boole.

        I believe that a systematic treatise on logic (or any other branch
of philosophy) by Mr. Peirce would be of permanent significance to the
philosophic world, and a very honorable addition to American science.

        I am, Sir,
                Yours very faithfully,

                    (signed) William Pepperell Montague

                Instructor in Logic and Theory of Knowledge,

                    University of California.

Berkeley, Calif.
  27 Dec. 1902.

[hand: Montague is not a great light & has come little in
contact with me. But I have done what I could for him, & I
believe he is now a professor in the Univ. of California]

                 COLUMBIA UNIVERSITY
                      DIVISION OF
        PHILOSOPHY, PSYCHOLOGY AND ANTHROPOLOGY

                         Garrison-on-Hudson, N.Y.   December 10, 1902


Dear Mr. Peirce:-

          I have read your letter with much interest.   Hyslop's
leaving had nothing to do with his spiritualism.   He is suffer-
ing from consumption and was given leave of absence last winter.
He returned this year, but found that he was unable to go on with
his work.

          As you know appointments in an American university
are almost a proprietory right of the president , and this would
hold especially in the case of a philosophical appointment at
Columbia.   I shall tell Butler that I wish that a place could
be made for you here, but I fear that it is not likely if only
because our professors are expected to retire at the age of sixty-
five.

          So far as I can see the affairs of the Carnegie Ins-
titution are not being managed very efficiently.   It is difficult,
however, to form a very correct opinion as everything they have
done or have not done has been kept as secret as possible.   The
secrecy, however, seems in itself deserving of criticism.

                              Very truly yours,

                                   [hand: J. McK. Cattell]


Mr. C. S. Peirce,
    Milford, Pa.

Professor Charles S. Peirce

My dear Prof. Peirce:-

                Your are quite right; you are the man that

ought to be supported by the Carnegie foundation until your logic

is finished, and I am going to see if I cannot bring it about.

I think I told you that George S. Morrison would be one of twelve

to give you one hundred dollars apiece each year, making twelve

hundred in all, until your logic was finished.  I will investigate

this matter pretty thoroughly, and then let you hear from me lat-

er.

        The other day I bought a MS. of Boethius on arithmetic,

written about 1200, and in the same volume was a complete MS. of

Euclid,- so when you come to the city you will have to see them

both.

        With my kind regards to Mrs. Peirce, believe me,

Dear Mr. Peirce:-
        I have read with much interest the copy of your ap-
plication to the executive committee of the Carnegie Institution,
which Dr. Jacobi handed to me.    I understand that the matter
is still under advisement by the committee.    I have written
urging as strongly as I can favorable action, as I regard the

                    70 Fifth Avenue,  New York,

                         January 27, 1902.

President Daniel C. Gilman,

              Johns Hopkins University,

                         Baltimore.

My dear President Gilman:

                         George S. Morrison, the great bridge-builder and engineer,

met me some time ago, and said that he would be glad to be one of twelve to give

$100. a year each to Charles S. Peirce, provided he would finish his book on

logic; that he considered that book of very great importance.  Now, why is not

Peirce just the sort of man to benefit by the Carnegie Endowment?  I should think

if he had enough to pay for his bread and butter, he would work like a Trojan on

this book until it was finished.  He has his peculiarities, but so have all other

geniuses that I have ever struck.  I wrote to his brother, James Mills Peirce,

of Harvard, and possibly you may have heard from him in regard to the matter.

I have seen a good deal of Charles S. Peirce, because he has worked for me in

my mathematical library, and he is a genius, if there ever was one.

                         Sincerely yours,

                              (signed)  Geo. A. Plimpton.

Clark University, Worcester, Mass.
                                   January 1st. 1903.

My dear Peirce:

                         As I understand that your brother Charles
has made application to the Carnegie Institution for aid in
bringing before the world the results of his studies in Logic
and philosophy, I desire herein to express my very decided
opinion of their importance and of the beneficial influence
they are likely to have upon the scientific work of the near
future when they shall be published, as well as my hearty
approval of any aid that may be given him for this purpose.
It is seldom that a student of metaphysics is so well fitted
by an intimate acquaintance with theoretical and practical
science of various kinds to make his metaphysics practically
useful as is your brother.  For over thirty years I have
known more or less of the work that {add}he{/add} happened to be doing;
I can truly say that it has never been my fortune to be
thrown into contact with anyone whose suggestions were more
valuable to me than his in my own department of pure mathe -
matics; and I believe that he has ideas that would materially
further the development of several sciences, if they were
known.  Indeed, I know that he has paid particular attention
to the logic of specific sciences for at least twenty years.
His published papers on logic, notably symbolic logic and
the logic of relatives, have established his reputation
throughout the world as an authority on that subject.  Of
his more purely philosophical work I do not feel competent
to speak.
          If what I have written can be of any assistance in sup-
port of his application, you are at liberty to make what use
of it you deem best.
                                   Sincerely yours,

                                             William E. Story.

Report of C. S. Peirce on investigation in Logic.
                                        [hand: check mark]

                         COPY.

                         Milford, Pa., April 12, 1902.

President of the Carnegie Institution,                              1-
     1439 K St.,
          Washington, D.C.

Dear Sir:
     I have received from the executive committee of the Carnegie
Institution a circular letter suggesting that any contribution from me
toward a conception of the work of the Institution will be gladly re-
ceived.  I think it my duty to respond to this in good faith.
[hand: bracket]     I have made some studies of the economy of research which
have led me to conclusions which seem to be pertinent.  In themselves,
they are not far from truisms;  but perhaps it is not equally self
evident that they ought to be regarded as the leading principles of the
economy of research.  If it is, it may be excusable to restate them.
If it seems doubtful, I will take advantage of the Committee's invi-
tation to urge that the propriety of adopting these principles as the
basis of the economy of research be taken into consideration.  I
cannot doubt that it will be agreed that some principles ought to be
recognised as having that relation to the work of the Institution.
     One important fact about research seems to me to be that,
on the average, a given expenditure of money,time,energy,genius, and
all that research costs produces a smaller and smaller increase of
knowledge the further the research is pushed.  If we examine al-
most any very simple case, where there appears to be nothing of an
exceptional character, we find this rule to hold.  Thus the probable

2.

error of the mean result of a number of measurements is inversely
proportional to the square root of the number of measures made; and
after the apparatus is once installed, the expense of every kind is
proportional to the number of measures made.    So a telescope which
will separate a double star whose components are 1" distant from one
another must cost something like a hundred times as much as one which
will separate components distant 10".    For the aperture will (other
things being equal) be ten times as great for the former as for the
latter and consequently the surface to be polished will be a hundred
times as great; and similar considerations will enter into estimates
of the majority of the factors of the expense.    No doubt exceptions
will readily be found; but a fair sample of examples will show approx-
imately the same law.    I only propose it as the average usual for-
mula.    Individual cases may violate it.
          Another general and quasi-statistical fact, although it is
a general principle of which the exceptions are still more numerous,
is that if we employ the word "useful" to mean useful for purely scien-
tific as well as for practical purposes,- valuable according to the
best and truest ethical scale,- then, on the average, a given increase
of knowledge becomes less and less "useful", or valuable, the more we
already know of the subject.
          From these two general facts taken together, it follows
that as a rule, having many exceptions, no doubt, yet with sufficient
truth to justify a certain prepossession or inclination of opinion,
newly opened studies, sciences in which fruitful and secure methods have
only recently been discovered, ought to be greatly preferred to maturer

3.

sciences for any kind of expenditure.  There is, therefore, no mistake
of policy into which an institution like yours can fall so harmful in
the long run as conservatism, or a tendency to encourage almost ex-
clusively long-approved sciences.

        But this is not to recommend the encouragement of an investi-
gation for which no advantageous methods of research have been dis-
covered.

        Another principle of great importance is that the more ab-
stract and general a branch of science, the more a genuine discovery
in that branch will be helpful to other sciences; and, therefore,
other things being at all near equal, mathematics is best worth ex-
penditure of every kind, philosophy next, and both nomological physics
and psychonomy before classificatory and still more before descriptive
sciences; while the useful arts need no encouragement under ordinary
conditions from such an institution.

[hand: bracket] I have held these opinions from an early date in my scientif-
ic life; and this is the reason why I early almost abandoned chemistry
the science in which I was specially trained, in order to follow up
studies in logic.

        If by a science we understand a study which has reached that
stage of maturity at which the fundamental methods are undisputed by
serious students, then logic and metaphysics are not yet sciences, al-
though both are apparently rapidly approaching that condition, logic
being in advance, as it must be, since metaphysics necessarily is based
upon the theory of logic.  Until a study reaches that stage of growth
at which it can be called a science in the above sense, there is
necessarily more or less wasted labor in the pursuit of it.  As long

4.

as it is very far from being yet pursued scientifically, it seems
better economy,- contrary to the general principle,- to expend means
of all kinds upon another study.  I believe that to be the truth in
large measure;  yet of a stage of groping, disputation, and fanciful
speculation is a condition precedent to the formation of those defi-
nite conceptions, there is a reason for desiring that that preliminary
activity should be exercised.  Taking the two considerations together
their resultant,- to use a dynamical phrase,- is that the energetic
encouragement of a study should commence as soon as it begins decided-
ly to approach the scientific stage.  This is the moment in the histo
ry of a science when the bold,originating genius is of inestimable
service.  At this point, those who have the funds of the Carnegie
Institution to distribute should consider two truths.  The first is
that this is the moment when money,- if it can be joined to other
factors that money cannot procure,- is worth a hundred times what it
will be a little later.  The second is, that since radically new con-
ceptions are, at this point, to be established, the ideas that are
really the ones which it is desirable to develop will necessarily
appear to all but the one man who is possessed of them to be utterly
wrong and wanting in good sense.  But you can find your genuine student
by other marks than those of his opinions.  If you find he is a
man of power, encourage his work.  If you find two such men of dia-
metrically opposite views (although diametrically opposite their
views can hardly be at bottom, when the study begins to become scien-
tific) then by all means encourage both.  Nothing has been of more
service in the history of science and of philosophy than the thorough
following out of erroneous ideas.  It is extremely often, one might
almost say usually, the only way in which the truth can be discovered.

5.

[hand: ✓]

I will now venture to submit to you certain considerations
in regard to these factors of scientific advance which money cannot
always procure, no matter how intelligently it be administered.  I
mean particularly Genius.  I have made some very serious and prolong-
ed studies of this subject, employing mathematical methods which I
should need almost the space of a book to explain clearly.  Since I
cannot set forth here my investigations, I naturally cannot expect or
desire that my conclusions should be definitely adopted.  Still, the
fact that they are the fruit of elaborate studies of history may lend
them some interest, if not some credit.  I did not bring these ideas
to the study: I was gradually forced to them by the study.

        My results are as follows: Nobody is born a great genius.
But certain men,perhaps one in ten million, are born with the capacity
of developing into a great genius.  Such persons have this great pe-
culiarity, namely, while a man capable of becoming a very eminent per-
son not a great genius, if he fails to do so, more often fails from
inward defects than from exterior difficulties, the infant born
capable of developing into a great genius but who fails so to develop,
always fails because outward circumstances must be such as they very
rarely are to enable him to attain that development of which he is
capable.  If these conclusions are correct, highly important corolla-
ries follow from them.  It follows that the reason why great geniuses
of a given description do not appear in a given state of society where
they do not in fact appear, is that there is not an effective demand for
them, and that if we knew how to go about the business, we could in
half a century produce any kind of a genius with as much certainty as
the bees grow a queen when a queen is needed.

6.

I would recommend the Carnegie Institution to take measures
to have my researches on this subject brought to light and studied,
so that, in case they should be {del}f{/del} and to be well founded, they should
at once be actively pushed further and ultimately carried to practical
methods.

For it is the business of the Carnegie Institution itself
to study one of the profoundest of sciences demanding great power of
mathematics, of logic, of philosophy, of psychology, of history,-
namely, the science of the Laws of {del}the{/del} Development of Science.

This science must be an offshoot of logic and must rest on a
sound general theory of logic.     I can myself aid in this part of the
work and should be willing to accept some assistance from the Carnegie
Institution to do so.     I ask for that assistance. I shall go no
further.

I do not suppose that those persons in charge of the Carne-
gie Institution feel competent themselves to undertake the scientific
study of the Laws of {del}the{/del} Development of Science.     But they ought to
recognize as one of their first duties the pursuit of that science.

The more mature a science becomes, the less is genius or
extraordinary natural power called for, and the more important becomes
training and technique.     Yet crises come in the developed science
where there is felt keenly the need of some very great men who is not
forthcoming.  Why is he not?  That is a question which the Carnegie
Institution should see gets answered in a practically useful way.

I will now presume to take advantage of the invitation of
your committee to offer two more suggestions.  One of these is that
while I have known great men of science who were by temperament and

[hand: check mark]

                                7.

discipline modest men, I never knew one who had the least doubt of the
value of his own researches,-- except that sort of critical doubt which
is a part of the sound scientific procedure; and unless a man is deep-
ly convinced himself of the value of his work, I dont believe he can do
anything demanding particular encouragement.

          In the next place, is it wise to draw an absolute hard and
fast line, and say we will encourage no science not of a given de-
scription?  It seems to me that so to narrow the sphere of the Car-
negie Institution would be to display the kind of mind that is given
to framing arbitrary rules, and not at all the wisdom that is needed
to develop all the great possibilities of this wonderful benefaction.
I can hardly believe, what I have nevertheless been positively informed
that a positive determination has been reached that none of the money
should be spent upon books.  That rule must at once contract the
circle of its utility very greatly.  Expensive books in large numbers/
beyond the probable means of the investigator, and quite beyond
what any public libraries or combination of public libraries can render
serviceable are indispensable in many branches of study, of which one
is Logic.  I know it by bitter experience.  Nothing, all my life
long, has so hampered my own work as the lack of books, although li-
braries have been most indulgent.  When I think of the cases where
I have been obliged from lack of books to abandon researches which
were going on prosperously and upon which all my energies were bent,
it makes me faint and ill.  It is most terrible to endure these mem-
ories.  I {del}not{/del} mention this merely in order to show how the prosecu-
tion of useful researches may be rendered absolutely impossible, and
the whole future of every science indirectly depending on those studies

[hand: ✓]

                                8.

may easily be dwarfed for centuries by a resolution of the Carnegie
Institution of hostility to books.  I do not think that Institution
ought to be hostile to any adjuvant of research of any kind.

                                    Very respectfully,

                                        (Signed) C. S. Peirce.

[hand: Jan 175 Typed duplicate]                      [hand: xii]

inference.  Abduction is iconic argument, induction is indicative
argument, deduction is symbolic argument.  This, of course, will
be accurately stated and fully explained in the memoir.  Nor is
methodeutic confined to the consideration of arguments.  On the
contrary, its special subjects have always been understood to
be the definition and division of terms.  The formation of systems
of propositions, although it has been neglected, should also
evidently be included in methodeutic.  In its method, methodeutic
is less strict than critic.

No. 28.  On the Economics of Research.  [hand: Start]  [hand: §2. Later Reflections]
     In all economics the laws are ideal formulae from which
there are large deviations, even statistically.  In the economics
of research the "laws" are mere general tendencies to which
exceptions are frequent.  The laws being so indefinite, at best,
there is little advantage in very accurate definitions of such
terms as "amount of knowledge".  It is, however, possible to
attach a definite conception to one increment of knowledge being
greater than another.  To work this out will be the first business
of the memoir.  I also establish a definite meaning for the
amount of an increment in diffusion of knowledge.  I then con-
sider the relation of each of these to the expenditure of energy
and value required to produce them in varying conditions of the
advancement or diffusion of knowledge already attained.  Comparing
knowledge with a material commodity, we know that in the latter
case a given small increment in the supply is very expensive, in
most cases, when the supply is very small, that as the supply
increases, it sinks to a minimum, from which it increases to a very

[hand: 96]

        large but finite value of the supply where no further increment
        would be possible at any finite cost.  Putting instead of supply,
        the amount of knowledge attained, we find that there is a "law",
        or general tendency, subject to similar large irregularities as
        in the case of the supply of a material commodity, but here even
        greater.  The final increase of cost of an increment with the
        increase of attainment already achieved is marked, on the whole,
        in almost all cases, while in many cases, at least, there is a
        point of attainment where the cost of an increment is at a
        minimum.  The same general tendency appears in reference to
        the diffusion of knowledge; but there is this striking difference,
        that attainments in advance of sciences are very commonly actually
        on the upward slope where increments are costing more and more,
        while there are few branches of knowledge whose diffusion is
        already so great that a given increment of the diffusion will cost
        more and more, as the diffusion is increased.
             I shall next pass to a study of the variation of the utility
        (meaning, generally, the scientific utility) of given small
        increments of scientific knowledge and of the diffusion of knowledge
        in varying states of attainment.  This is to be compared with the
        variation of the total amount that will be paid for a commodity
        for a fixed small increment of the demand, or amount thrown upon
        the market to fetch what it will, with varying amounts of that
        demand.  Here, the additional total amount that will be paid for
        the small increment of amount sold will correspond to the utility
        of the small fixed increase of scientific knowledge or of the
        diffusion of knowledge; while, the demand being equal to the

[hand: 77]

supply, this demand, or total amount that is sold, will corres-
pond as before to the amount of attainment in scientific know-
ledge or in the diffusion of knowledge.  For a material commodity
we know that if it is given away people will only carry home a
finite amount.  One would have to pay them to carry away more.
On the other hand there is probably some maximum price for most
things, above which none at all would be sold.  It necessarily
follows that beyond a certain amount thrown upon the market, a
small increment in that amount would actually diminish the total
receipts from the sale of it, while for any smaller amount the
increment of receipts for a given small increment of amount sent
to market would be less and less.  With regard to the scientific
utility of a small fixed advance of knowledge, the "law" is
certainly very different from that.  In the first place, there
is no degree of knowledge of which a small increase would be
worse than useless, and while the general tendency is that the
utility of such fixed increase becomes less and less, yet this
curve is rather saw shaped, since like Rayleigh's small addition
to our knowledge of the density of nitrogen, now and then a small
increment will be of great utility and will then immediately
sink to its former level.  The scientific advantage of the
diffusion of knowledge is difficult to determine.  It cannot be
believed that any increment of diffusion is positively unfavorable
to science.  It is favorable in two ways; first, by preparing
more men to be eminent researchers; and secondly, by increasing
general wealth, and therefore the money bestowed on science.  I am

[hand: 18]

inclined to think that the general tendency is that a given
increment of diffusion is less and less advantageous to science
the greater the attained diffusion.  But I am not confident that
this is so, at any rate without very important deflexions.  The
general effect, however, is nearly the same for the advancement
as for the diffusion of knowledge.  Namely, beginning with dense
ignorance, the first increments cost more than they come to.
That is, knowledge is increased but scientific energy is spent
and not at once recovered.  But we very soon reach a state of
knowledge which is profitable to science, that is, not only is
knowledge increased, but the facility of increasing knowledge
gives us a return of more available means for research than we
had before the necessary scientific energy was spent.  This
increases to a maximum, diminishes, and finally, there is no
further gain.  Yet still, in the case of energy expended upon
research, if it is persisted in, a fortunate discovery may
result in a new means of research.  I shall analyze as far as I
can the relative advantages, for pure science exclusively, of
expending energy (which is of such a kind as to be equally
capable of being directed either way) to the direct advancement
of knowledge and to the diffusion of knowledge.  I find the latter
so overwhelmingly more important (although all my personal
sympathies are the other way) that it appears to me that, for the
present, to give to research, in money, one or two per cent of
what is spent upon education is enough.  Research must contrive
to do business at a profit; by which I mean that it must produce
more effective scientific energy than it expends.  No doubt, it

already does so.  But it would do well to become conscious of
its economical position and contrive ways of living upon it.

     Many years ago I published a little paper on the Economy of
Research, in which I considered this problem.*  Somebody furnishes
a fund to be expended upon research without restrictions.  What
sort of researches should {add}it{/add} be expended upon?  My answer, to
which I still adhere, was this.  Researches for which men have
been trained, instruments procured, and a plant established,
should be continued while those conditions subsist.  But the
new money should mainly go to opening up new fields; because new
fields will probably be more profitable, and, at any rate, will
be profitable longer.

     I shall remark in the course of the memoir that economical
science is particularly profitable to science; and that of all
the branches of economy, the economy of research is perhaps the
most profitable; that logical methodeutic and logic in general
are specially valuable for science, costing little beyond the
energies of the researcher, and helping the economy of every
other science.  It was in the middle of the 13th century that a
man distinguished enough to become pope opened his work on logic
with the words, "Dialectica est ars artium et scientia
scientiarum, ad omnium methodorum principia viam habens".  This
memorable sentence, whose gothic ornamentation proves upon
scrutiny to involve no meaningless expression nor redundant
clause, began a work wherein the idea of this sentence was
executed satisfactorily enough for the dominant science of the
middle ages.  Jevons adopted the sentence as the motto of his

[hand: *[U.S. Coast Survey Report for 1876, pp. 197-201]]

most scientific contribution to logic; and it would express the
purpose of my memoirs, which is, upon the ground well prepared
by Jevons and his teacher De Morgan, and by the other great
English researchers, especially Boole, Whewell, Berkeley, Glanvill,
Ockham, and Duns Scotus, to lay a solid foundation upon which
may be erected a new logic fit for the life of twentieth century
science.

[hand: End]

No. 29.  On the Course of Research.
        Comparing the two wings of the special sciences, i.e.,
Psychognosy and Physiognosy, and taking the history of their
development as a basis, but correcting the history, as well as
we can, in order to make it conform to what good logic and good
economy would have made it, we get the idea of rational courses
of development which those branches might have followed.  Between
these two there is a striking parallel; so that we can formulate
a general rational course of inquiry.  Now passing to the study
of the history of special sciences, also modified by the same
process, we find some traces of the same law; or to express it
more clearly, it is as if in the special science showed us one
part of the general scheme under a microscope.  By successively
examining all the sciences in this way (or all I am sufficiently
able to comprehend), we can fill in details, and make the general
formula more definite.  We find here a succession of conceptions
which we can generalize in some measure, but which we find it
difficult to generalize very much without losing their peculiar
"flavors".  These I call the Categories of the course of research.
