Explanation of what the proposed work is.

I imbibed the spirit of positive science, and especially that
of exact science, from my boyhood. I early became most in-
tensely curious concerning the theory of the methods of science;
and {add}shortly after{/add} {del}upon{/del} my graduation from college, in 1859, determined to
devote my life to that study; although indeed it was less a resolve
than an overmastering passion, which for several years I had
been unable to check. It has never abated. In 1866 and especially
in 1867, I entered upon my first original contributions to the
science of logic. I have continued my studies of this science ever
since, with a very few vacations of a few months each. Owing
to my treating logic as a science and making my researches
special, minute, exact, and checked by experience, and
owing to the fact that logic had very rarely been studied
before in that way, discoveries have poured in upon me
in a flood which was quite embarrassing. This has been
one reason why I have published only a few fragments of

[hand: Application
of C.S. Peirce
3]

[hand: red bracket in left margin]

my work. For logic differs from the natural sciences, the
psychical sciences, and even in some measure from
mathematics, in being essentially systematic. Conse-
quently, if {del}new{/del} new discoveries were made in the course
of writing a paper, they might easily require the whole to
be remodelled, involving a very mature reconsideration
of the whole. Still, no important result which I have ever
reached has required more than modification in details;
at the most, the relative importance of principles has had
to be altered, and this but seldom. But what has chiefly
prevented my publishing much has been, first, that my
desire to teach has not been so strong as my desire to learn,
and secondly, that so far from there being any demand for
papers by me, I have found considerable difficulty in getting
them printed as a favor to myself. But now I am getting
to be an old man, going on to 63 years of age, and I feel
that I should not be doing what I was put into the world to

[hand: Application
C.S. Peirce
4]

do, if I did not now spend all my forces available in working
up for publication as many of my logical results as I can.
What I desire to do, therefore, is to prepare some three dozen
memoirs, each complete in itself, yet forming together
a complete and unitary system of logic, which shall present,
in a manner absolutely convincing to a scientific mind, the
results to which I have arrived, with clear explanations of
how it is that other logicians have been led to opine dif-
ferently; the whole putting logic, for the first time, upon
an undeniably scientific footing.

every variety. Critic has no direct bearing upon
terms, upon Analytic, or Explicatory, Propositions, nor

of fallacious demonstrations and of probable deductions.
Methodeutic has no direct bearing upon any terms
or propositions or {add}upon{/add} any kind of reasoning except
that which starts hypotheses. After Critical Logic has

hypotheses to be considered. No such supplementary inquiry
is called for {del}[illegible]{/del} in the case of a deductive or {add}any{/add} inductive
conclusion. Indirectly, however, methodeutic treats of all

The history of divisions of logic is examined; and
it is shown that my division has been virtually quite
generally approved, except so far as it has been deranged

Dr. Carus says it is irregularity. Mr. Venn says it is isolation. Both
these opinions can be decisively refuted. Another theory, that a
phenomenon demands explanation just in so far as it is surpri-

                Concerning the Utility of the Work.

It appears to me that almost any man of 62, who
is endowed with reason, is a better judge of his own
powers and of the utility of his own work than other
{del}people{/del} can be. This is particularly the case when he
has accumulated a large store of unpublished results.
Yet when such a man is in the position of seeking
recognition for the utility of his work, he comes into
a situation in which suspicions may be entertained
as to the candor of his appreciation, or may be sug-
gested by those who are unfavorable to the action
he desires.

        I shall, therefore, confine myself to stating in
a general way my profound conviction of the utility
of publishing my results, as likely to influence
some sciences, but still more as themselves sti-
mulating a most important branch of science,

Estimate of the Labor required for the Work.
It would be better not to do the work than to do it ill.
My results in each of the three dozen topics have to be
carefully revised, although that has often been done
before, set into logical order and be presented in the
fully convincing form which they merit. It is also
most desirable that the presentation of each should
be as brief and as closely confined to what is pertinent
as {del}possible{/del} is consistent with completeness and per-
spicuity. A certain amount of labour, therefore, must
be bestowed upon their literary polish. Devoting my
whole energies to the work, as I should be obliged
to do, for when my mind is fully concentrated upon

[hand: Application C.C.S. Peirce 4 p 41+]

logic, I cannot turn aside, but become an idiot for
other purposes, six memoirs a year would be all
I could promise. I should expect to average a little
more; and since all are not equally laborious, in
some years I might do nine. I think I should finish
the work in five years. I have considerable experience
which gives me confidence in this estimate.

I do not want to inflict as many as a million
words upon a student; but I am quite sure that
the whole subject covered could not usefully be
brought within half a million. My aim will be,
therefore, at the sacrifice of good matter, to bring
each memoir as nearly down to 20000 words as
possible. I fear all will overrun; and some will
be two or three times that length. I shall therefore
come {del}prob{/del} nearer the million than I should wish.

Committee may receive suggestions that it is indo-
lence which I am scheming for. But I am willing
to agree to send on with {add}each{/add} {del}the memoirs{/del} papers {add}written{/add}
in the preparation of it showing that it is the result
of condensation to from 1/3 to 1/5 of what I was pre-
pared to say; and that I have actually written 2000
words a day (which is my steady habit at all times.)
When one takes into consideration the amount of care-
ful reading almost every memoir involves, to say
nothing of the intellectual labor of revising my
results and putting them into shape and logical
order, I do not think that anybody will think it
wise to endeavor to persuade the Executive Com-
mittee that indolence is my characteristic.

[hand: Application of C.S.Peirce
42
43]

Estimate of Other Expense involved.

It is desirable that during this work I should occasionally -
see something of scientific men and students of phi-
losophy. That is, however, not indispensible.
But what is indispensible is that what is said should
be said convincingly, and therefore that due notice should
be paid to opposing opinions. For this purpose, books
must be criticized. Now no matter how familiar one may
be with a book, one must have it at hand in order to
venture upon any remark about it, except the most
general. There are other books which are absolutely
indispensible for this work. I should have to add 500
volumes to my present library of some 2000. They would
cost me {del}$2000{/del}. I might perhaps obtain the use of them
for five years by agreeing to surrender my whole library
at the end of that period, It is true that I could then no
longer give students the advantage of my instruction, as I

[hand: Application
C.S. Peirce
44]

like to do, and my earnings are so dependent on my
books that such a step would be a dernier resort. Every-
day duties must come first; but after them {add}my{/add} {del}the{/del} supreme
effort will be to give the world the results of my logical
studies. However, I do not know that I could make
such an arrangement.

                Need of the Aid asked for.

I am bound to confess that I should still have faith
that I should manage to give my results to the world,
even if the Carnegie Institution should refuse all coöpe-
ration. I am satisfied that {del}that{/del} faith is logically jus-
tified. It may prove mistaken; and if so, provided I shall
have done my part, I shall not be unhappy over it.
But while I fully believe I shall succeed, I certainly cannot
see how I am to do so in default of the aid which I ask
from the Carnegie Institution. It seems to be absolutely needful.

[hand: Application
of C.S.Peirce
48]

Suggested Plan for the requisite Aid.
I would suggest that each memoir when completed
should be sent by me in MS. or type-written to the
office of the Carnegie Institution and should be submitted
to the judgment of a qualified person, in order that he
should report whether it represented the expected amount
of work and thought; and that upon his favorable report
the treasurer of the Carnegie Institution should {del}pay{/del} remit
{add}to me{/add}
a certain sum, say so much for a memoir of
20000 words, and double that amount if the length
exceeded 40000. Or the amount might be invariable;
or it might be strictly proportionate to the number of
words. I have usually been paid $25 a thousand words
for such philosophical writing as I have been paid for.
Of course, none of it was nearly as laborious as this will be.
The amount would have to be sufficient for the support
of myself and wife, and for the purchase of some books.

[hand: Application
of C.S. Peirce
4/8
46]

There might be a limit as to the amount of work
receivable in any one year. The memoirs could be
published separately, and could be sold for the benefit of
the Carnegie Institution.

Since the {del}books{/del} needed are needed at the very outset,
although it would be far better that the memoirs should
be prepared in their intended order of {del}[unclear: some]{/del} consecution,
as numbered above, yet in order to enable me to obtain
the needed money for the books, I might the first year
produce such memoirs as could be most quickly pro-
duced, and might thus make nine or ten. This would be
a bad plan; but since I am informed that the Executive
Committee will, under no circumstances aid in fur-
nishing books required for the work done under their
auspices, it seems to remain the only feasible plan.
In case my information should be incorrect, if the Car-
negie Institution will provide me with $2000 worth of books
for a term of years, my whole library shall go to a school
of logic, or some arrangement shall be made agreeable to the committee.

[hand: Application
of C.S.Peirce
48]

Probability of the Completion of the Work.

Each memoir is complete in itself. But Logic is a
science which will not be completed until the sciences
of Biology and of History become complete. That the
series of three dozen should be completed is eminently
desirable. I am in excellent health and in capital trim for
work, and there is every prospect that I shall continue
so for at least as long as required to complete the pro-
jected series of memoirs. In case I should be cut off, I
should leave such papers that an appendix would supply
a pretty good notion of the unpublished memoirs.

I understand that it is thought that I have a
disposition not to persist in my undertakings. I admit
I have sometimes projected schemes which I did not carry
out for one reason or another, especially because I have
had comparatively small interest in anything but logic
